body: Ugh, then what? Get the offset from a fully-sized local dummy, then ptr-add on the real thing?
author: cuviper

author: tavianator

body:
My impression was that such use was the very *purpose* of `addr_of!` / `&raw`, and it seems I wasn't the only one from the discussion on #64490 and #73394. But that was left to the future in [RFC 2582](https://rust-lang.github.io/rfcs/2582-raw-reference-mir-operator.html#other).

It seems really unfortunate here since the pointer at hand is not entirely dangling, just _incomplete_ at the end, beyond the pointer we want to compute. A `MaybeUninit` local feels like such a waste, but should be optimized away. Maybe that can even be done in a true `const` these days?

I'm not aware of any existing `offset_of!`, so open-coding it seems fine, with a comment that it _could_ use `offset_of!` so we can find and update it later. :)
author: cuviper

body:
`addr_of!` is a weaker from of `&`, but it does nothing about `*`. The code here is UB due to the requirement of `*ptr` to only be used on pointers that point to actual memory (with the required size and alignment given by its type).

I would love to just entirely remove that restriction, but then we would have to change our LLVM codegen to use `getelementptr` instead of `getelementptr inbounds` in some situations.
author: RalfJung

body:
> Maybe that can even be done in a true const these days?

With some unstable features, it can -- see the https://github.com/Gilnaa/memoffset crate
author: RalfJung

body:
> I would love to just entirely remove that restriction, but then we would have to change our LLVM codegen to use getelementptr instead of getelementptr inbounds in some situations.

This is probably worth doing, honestly. I can't imagine the use of `gepi` over `gep` wins us almost anything on modern hardware (IIUC it mostly just allows folding the address arithmetic into a subsequent load), and even if it does, we still would be able to use it in all cases except for with addr_of.

body: I don't know which of these is technically more correct, but I know that I find the version before this PR to be easier to read/understand. I read "panics" as a plural noun by default ("one panic, two panics") and need to restart reading the sentence when that isn't what it means (such as this case), whereas "panicks" is unambiguously third-person singular simple present ("it panicked, it was panicking, it panicks often"). Since Rust commonly uses both noun and verb meaning of panic, I think it would be fine for us to use "panicks" for the verb meaning.
author: dtolnay

body: @dtolnay As far as I can tell, we more-or-less consistently use "panics" as a verb almost everywhere (since most of the time we don't have to deal with more than one panic at once). "panicks" would be inconsistent (in addition to not being correct in English).

body:
For symmetry with most other enum variants, most notably `Module(Module)`, this should be `StrippedModule(StrippedModule)`.

Another option that I find even more attractive since it removes the need for more types and enum variants is to simply add  `is_stripped` to `struct Module`, so it becomes:

```rust
pub struct Module {
    pub is_crate: bool,
    pub is_stripped: bool,
    pub items: Vec<Id>,
}
```

That would be even more symmetric with existing code, since there is no `CrateModule` (which would be an alternative to having the current `is_crate` field in `Module`).

body:
You should be able to improve this in one of two ways:
* split the `()` case from the default:
  * `()` is "guaranteed simple" (but maybe specializing on the original pointee being `Sized` would be stronger)
  * the default would only be hit by custom DSTs (and unreachable today? could print some indication of metadata existing but not being understood, or just fit an `unreachable!()` in there)
* add a new **sealed** trait, and a bound for it on `Pointee::Metadata` (just like it requires `Copy`, `Eq`, etc. today)
  *  ("sealed" as in a `pub trait` in a private `mod`, not nameable outside `core` - for now, anyway)
  * the new trait could just be this PR's `PtrMetadataFmt` for now, even if we may want to change it in the future (see below)
  * no specialization should be needed, since `Pointee` would be claiming it's always implemented for valid metadata types
  * whenever we get custom DSTs, it would be a nice bonus to force the user to implement such a trait, effectively making them decide how raw pointers to their custom DST should be formatted
    * this is when we'd remove the "sealing"
    * maybe it should just be a `fmt::Debug` bound and not let the user see the data pointer or choose how *it* is printed?
    * but this part is really all a bikshed for the future

But the RFC specifies a different behavior, indices are counted from inside out, starting with `0`.

>The meaning of the depth parameter in index and count originally counted inwards from the outer-most nesting. This was changed to count outwards from the inner-most nesting so that expressions can be copied to a different nesting depth without needing to change them.

body:
This is a bit strange because https://github.com/markbt/rfcs/blob/macro_metavar_expr/text/0000-macro-metavar-expr.md#count explicitly says that `count` is to referring outer-most values.

> The three values this expands to are the number of outer-most repetitions (the number of times a would be generated), the sum of the number of middle repetitions (the number of times b would be generated), and the total number of repetitions of $x.

While `index` and `length` are referring to inner-most values (https://github.com/markbt/rfcs/blob/macro_metavar_expr/text/0000-macro-metavar-expr.md#index-and-length).

> The three values this expands to are the number of outer-most repetitions (the number of times a would be generated), the sum of the number of middle repetitions (the number of times b would be generated), and the total number of repetitions of $x.

In any case, `count` now stars at `0` instead of `1`. The old behavior was indeed confusing.

I left a bunch of comments in various places. My main concerns:

> which of the PR implements ignore, I don't see where variables used inside ${...} are marked as used, but apparently the variables used inside ignore are indeed counted as used because there's no warning.

If you mean linting for unused meta-variables, I don't think we have any. It might be a good lint to add...

EDIT: I mean it doesn't exist for macros in general, and we should add it as a separate feature in a different PR.

If you mean checking for the number of iterations (e.g. so that `$(${ignore(x)} a)*` outputs `a` the number of times `$x` matched), that is done by `lockstep_iter_size`. The call to `lockstep_iter_size` from `transcribe` gets the number of repetitions matched by all variables repeating at that depth (and checks that all variables repeat the same amount).

body: `depth_opt` is the user provided input index of `count($some_ident, some_user_input)`, which differs from the actual declared number of "nested repetitions". For example, given `$( { $( $foo:ident )* } )*` and `$( $( count(foo, 99999) )* )*`, `depth_opt` would be 99999 while the maximum number number of "nested repetitions" is 2 (count depth can then be only 0 or 1)

body:
Heh, I really tried my best on these examples :)

Maybe it is easier to understand this way: `count` counts every element repetition of every nested loop while `length` only counts the repetition of a single loop.

For example, given 5 nested loops declared on the LHS and a macro call specifying `2` outermost repetitions, `${length(4)}` [index `4` because there are 5 nested loops (`length` starts in the innermost loop)] will output `2` and `${count(ident, 0)}` [index `0` because `count` starts in the outermost loop (same as `${count(ident)}`)] will output the sum of all `ident` (whatever token) repetitions of all `5` nested loops (could be 10, 2304 or 999999999, idk).

body: Could you explain what this negative offset does? I have trouble understanding how this code manages to find the correct indentation.
author: cjgillot

body:
The top of https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.58.1/compiler/rustc_ast_pretty/src/pp.rs has an overview of the printing algorithm. Very briefly, when you make a sequence of cbox string break string break string break string end calls on the printer, either all of those breaks will turn into linebreaks, or none of them will turn into linebreaks. If they turn into linebreaks, the indentation at each one is the sum of the indentation of all the surrounding boxes. So if we have: 
ibox(4)cbox(0)Struct {breaka: Abreakb: Bbreak}endend
and it does not fit on the line, then you'd expect to get: ```console Struct { a: A b: B } ``` That's why every break also holds its own offset which is relative to the sum of the surrounding boxes' indents, so that we can set -4 on the last break and get: ```console Struct { a: A b: B } ```

body:
I'm surprised this code does as much dubious spinning as it does. In practice most of it you'd expect not to hurt that badly (which matches my experience with crossbeam), but on some arches a few of the placements of `backoff.spin()` could have bad behavior.

The spinlock was a surprise though. I had no idea crossbeam had one.
author: thomcc

body: `1 << 6`  calls to `hint::spin_loop()` is... very aggressive. Those calls tend to be a few hundred cycles. Actually, it's `1 << 6 + 1 << 5 + 1 << 4 + ...` before it falls back to pinging thread::yield(), which is not really ideal either — "switch to random thread" is almost always bad behavior in real code.
author: thomcc

body:
It's an antipattern to spin in the failure case of a compare_exchange_weak loop like this (there are a few others too in this code). For those you should just update the start var and continue to retry immediately. There are a lot of reasons doing it the way you have is bad:

1. The code just got an updated `head` value right before waiting. The best time to use it was right after getting the updated value, not later. Waiting will increase the likelihood that that value becomes stale, especially as the spin count gets higher.

    If you look at the asm on arm, you can see that in these cases it could easly just repeatedly end up repeatedly doing ldrex, bailing out and clrex+spinning, without ever getting to even *try* the strex https://rust.godbolt.org/z/aYYEKd6rE

2. You're basically not allowed to do anything complex inside the ll/sc loop so in practice calling the backoff here makes the code much worse as it doubles-up the loop to manage this.

3. The compare_exchange was weak so it may have a totally spurious failure anyway.

4. Not an issue here, but it can cause livelock if you're unlucky and the backoff count and atomic are on the same line.

In practice most people should use AtomicFoo::fetch_update for this stuff which gets all of this right, and is less tedious, and expresses intent better too. That said, this loop does enough that it might be hard to make that way.

The other usage function might be okay -- or, I don't love it (it seems like it would just make things worse here... is there a specific situation that was trying to be avoided), but this one is wrong.

(I will note that the implementations that are cited as reference don't have it, and it's surprising to see something like this, since I think it doesn't actually have to wait for other threads to make progress...)

author: jonhoo

body:
There is actually a good reason for spinning on CAS failure: if a thread encounters a lot of CAS failures then it means that the atomic it is trying to access is heavily contended. In such a situation where multiple threads are trying to access the same cacheline, you can significantly improve throughput by backing off and letting a single thread access the atomic at a time.

Essentially the access patterns go from:
```
Thread A
Thread B
Thread A
Thread B
Thread A
Thread B
Thread A
Thread B
```
to:
```
Thread A
Thread A
Thread A
Thread A
Thread B
Thread B
Thread B
Thread B
```

This improves throughput since the cacheline stays on the same CPU across multiple accesses.

This optimization looks good in benchmarks (I do something similar in parking_lot) but it is unclear how much impact it has on real world programs. I don't even know where to begin measuring this.

However actually I think @riking you are wrong. `let _ = *ptr;` is a NOP. `let _ = expr;` is only equivalent to `drop(expr)` when `expr` is not a pure place expression. `self.inner` is a place expression, so this compiles to nothing.

If you want a destructor to be executed, just call `drop`.

body:
That's just a 128bit vector which fits into a single XMM register. And since that is the lower half of the YMM register in AVX code, that probably just happens to work by accident on x86_64.

I guess using an x86 target with code that has the SSE target feature enabled on only foo or bar might fail (careful, IIRC the i686 arch enables SSE2bby default). Or maybe some of the other supported archs can trigger a bug here as well.
author: dotdash

body: The i586 targets disable SSE by default I believe. You can also manually disable it.
author: bjorn3

body: @dotdash Yes and no, above this code there is a check for the max size by value who is ptr-size * 2, so the created vector cannot be (at least on x86_64) upper than 128bits which as you said fits on the lower half of the YMM register. This is also consistent with clang++ who (accordingly to my test) never pass vectors more than ptr-size * 2 by value.
author: Urgau

body:
@Urgau that's because the SysV x86_64 specifies it like that. But the Rust ABI is (currently) non-arch specific so it has to work for every supported arch. That the 128bit limit makes this work for x86_64 is (as far as I'm concerned) by accident, because that limit is not there to make this work, but to limit the size of aggregates that get passed in registers by any means. It's meant to make a tradeoff between stack and register usage in function calls.

clang++ using the SysV x86_64 ABI will happily pass a vector of more than 128bits by value if you ask for such a vector explicitly (https://godbolt.org/z/ejcfKKW5j). It just doesn't automatically promote any aggregate to such large vectors because that would violate the ABI.

author: Urgau

body:
This is a breaking change. This would break returning a struct of floats using the rust abi if SSE is disabled and soft-float isn't enabled. In that case LLVM will refuse to use the xmm registers, but the abi requires it to use them, so it throws an error.

```rust
pub struct f32x4(f32, f32, f32, f32);

pub extern "C" fn foo() -> f32x4 {
    f32x4(1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0)
}
```

gives

```
error: <source>:5:2: in function _ZN7example3foo17h753a09441bc16561E { double, double } (): SSE register return with SSE disabled
```

when compiled with `-Ctarget-feature=-sse`. https://rust.godbolt.org/z/f4Gehcnx7 This PR changes the rust abi to do the exact same thing. While you could argue that `-sse` should be used in combination with `+soft-float` and that returning a bare float doesn't work either before this PR, it is still technically a breaking change.
author: bjorn3

body:
@bjorn3 I don't really follow, the example you gave use `extern "C"` but my PR only modify the Rust ABI.
And if I test your example with the Rust ABI and my changes it compiles with both `-Ctarget-feature=-sse` and `-Ctarget-feature=-sse,+soft-float`, so I don't think their is any breaking changes.

body:
If I follow the docs on Try correctly, I think this isn't quite right.

`Try::from_output(x).branch() --> ControlFlow::Continue(x)` to me implies that `type Output = ExitStatus` would be correct here.

I think the current implementation is forcing a synthesis of ExitStatus from (), whereas you actually "want" to just pass along the ExitStatus you're taking in `branch`.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
This is definitely the least straightforward part of this patch, and definitely deserves scrutiny.
I believed though that it does meet the `Try` laws, because `branch() --> ControlFlow::Continue(x) iff self.code() == 0`.

And I think `type Output = ExitStatus` seems weird to me, because `ExitStatusError` is a subset of `ExitStatus` such that the values intersect, so I felt it was weird in the way that a `Result<ExitStatus, ExitStatusError>` would be strange, since it has values which would be valid in both the `Ok`, and the `Err` branches.

Anyhow, that is the justification for why I did it this way at least.
author: ratmice

body:
I also noticed this in the documentation for the `Residual` associated type:
> This represents the possible values of the Self type which are not represented by the Output type.

https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/std/ops/trait.Try.html#associatedtype.Residual

Which `type Output = ExitStatus` would run afoul of.

Edit: I think if we want to change the `type Output` here away from unit, we should try and introduce a new type to complement `ExitStatusError`.  Such as `ExitStatusSuccess` or some bikeshed to that extent.

Edit 2: This would also would allow `exit_status????` because success would be identity which implements `Try`.

body: Hm, this seems suspicious -- I would expect us to change bootstrap to enable llvm-libunwind by default for Fuchsia in config.rs (roughly), is there a reason that's not the approach used here?
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
That's what I wanted to do originally, but it's not the way things were structured for other linux to begin with. Instead the meaning of "no" changes depending on the platform configuration being built.

```
# Use LLVM libunwind as the implementation for Rust's unwinder.
# Accepted values are 'in-tree' (formerly true), 'system' or 'no' (formerly false).
# This option only applies for Linux and Fuchsia targets.
# On Linux target, if crt-static is not enabled, 'no' means dynamic link to
# `libgcc_s.so`, 'in-tree' means static link to the in-tree build of llvm libunwind
# and 'system' means dynamic link to `libunwind.so`. If crt-static is enabled,
# the behavior is depend on the libc. On musl target, 'no' and 'in-tree' both
# means static link to the in-tree build of llvm libunwind, and 'system' means
# static link to `libunwind.a` provided by system. Due to the limitation of glibc,
# it must link to `libgcc_eh.a` to get a working output, and this option have no effect.
```

Untangling this doesn't sound like a particularly fun time tbh. I think I would have structured the config differently but that will probably require a breaking change and changelog entry.

That being said, I'm fine with doing it your preferred way for Fuchsia in this PR and letting a future PR make Linux more consistent.
author: tmandry

body: Yeah, I agree it's a bit of a mess. I'd prefer we try to decrease it rather than increase it, and I think moving the platform-dependency into bootstrap is probably the way to go. Starting with Fuchsia and dealing with Linux (which as you say, is more complicated) later makes sense to me.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

Should I add something like

author: Kobzol

body:
> (which has to be done in a platform-dependent manner)

That's probably confusing. We're going from [platform-dependent byte-representation, platform-independent value] to [platform-independent byte-representation, platform-dependent value]. Which means all operations that depend on the value must happen before that and afterwards we could only do bit-twiddling operations.
It would be more obvious if we used `to_le_bytes`.

I don't mean to explain endianness, it's just about which things must be be done before and after the conversion. That's what I didn't consider during the review. 😓
author: the8472

body:
I find `.to_le()` and `.to_be()` to be really confusing and always use `to_le_bytes()` and `to_be_bytes()` instead, which makes it much less likely to get things accidentally wrong (by converting twice for example).

Now that we have const generics it would probably be easy to just change `SipHasher128::short_write()` to `SipHasher128::short_write<const LEN: usize>(&mut self, bytes: &[u8; LEN])`.

However if people feel it's strongly associated with `&mut self` I'm open to a better name, I'd just like to hear some suggestions on alternatives or a feedback on my own ides for alternatives (see above).

body:
I do see the parallel, though to me this API doesn't do the "and replacing it with something" part that distinguishes `take` from `into_inner` kinds of things.

But more importantly to me, `mem::take(&mut my_box)` works today, so I think `Box::take` doing something pretty different would be surprising.  If anything, I might expect it to be `mem::take(&mut *my_box)` -- note the deref -- like how `RefCell::take` works.  (And note that `mem::take(&mut my_option)` does exactly the same thing as `Option::take(&mut my_option)`, as another example.)

body:
We might want to be nudging people towards larger unsafe blocks rather than to maximally small ones.

In unsafe code maintaining safety invariants will typically involve multiple independent statements, and at least some style guides suggest including both the unsafe operation itself as well any preceding statements that contribute to it being safe (e.g. a null check) into the same unsafe block.

author: steffahn

body:
Well, I personally much prefer more fine-grained unsafe blocks. Otherwise it’s often quite hard to identify where _exactly_ the unsafe operations actually are and whether or not one might have overlooked one of them on review. AFAIK, there’s often no clear boundary as to which operations are and which aren’t relevant to maintaining safety invariants. And often even the behavior of “unrelated” safe operations (e.g. ones that can access private fields of a data structure you’re implementing) can be highly relevant for overall soundness.

Also the behavior as implemented makes for a straightforward solution of the problem that cases such as the one fixed in f3a599222b3b6b60b1f293a17100bb946839988d weren’t reported previously, or similarly cases such as the one I described [above](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/93678#issuecomment-1030685757):


> On current nightly,
> 
> ```rust
> unsafe fn unsf() {}
> 
> fn inner_ignored() {
>     unsafe {
>         #[allow(unused_unsafe)]
>         unsafe {
>             unsf()
>         }
>     }
> }
> ```
> 
> doesn’t create any warnings.

While the case with an inner `#[allow(unused_unsafe)]` is detectable, and actually that’s what I tried to do initially when working on this PR, for the external-macro case I’d need to find out how to detect those… maybe even non-external macros, too, because it always makes little sense to get a warning that suggests removing an `unsafe` block that’s coming from a macro. OTOH, special-casing warning-behavior on macros seems weird, I don’t know if there’s precedent (for non-external macros).

<hr>

Also, considering that while `unsf()` is unsafe Rust code, wrapping it in an `unsafe` block masks the unsafety, making `unsafe { unsf() }` a safe expression, so then it would make the most sense to me that in the expression `unsafe { unsafe { unsf() } }`, the outer `unsafe` is the unused one.

Really, this `unused_unsafe` lint combines two different kinds of lints, one for an `unsafe` that’s truly unused (as in, it only contains safe code, and `unsafe { unsf() }` is safe code), and another one for detecting `unsafe` blocks within other `unsafe` blocks, where the inner `unsafe` block is ***not*** unused but still *redundant*, because it’s appearing in a context where unsafe code would have been allowed. Of course, these two lints together can – if one is not careful – create bad overall suggestions (suggesting to remove too many `unsafe` blocks). The way to resolve this taken after this PR is to consider the warning about *actually* “unused” `unsafe` blocks to take precedent, and then the warning for redundant `unsafe` blocks within other `unsafe` blocks only triggers if the containing `unsafe` block turns out not to be unused. In my opinion it makes sense if a lint called `unused_unsafe` is mainly concerned with actually unused `unsafe` blocks.

Suggesting removal of the inner `unsafe` block in the expression `unsafe { unsafe { unsf() } }` instead would give precedent to the warning about the inner `unsafe` block being redundant and then, to avoid inconsistent suggestions/warnings, act as if the `unsf()` contained within that block actually used the outer `unsafe` block.

***So… well… of course, ultimately it stays question of mainly personal opinion on which kind of warning is preferred.***

Note that the case that one unused `unsafe` block contains *multiple* other/inner `unsafe` blocks is in an edit-distance kind of way closer to code with lots of small `unsafe` blocks only, compared to code with a single big `unsafe` block only. Warning only on the outer `unsafe` block creates fewer warnings and (implicitly) suggests fewer changes to the code. A piece of code that already perfectly contains an exhaustive set of smaller `unsafe` blocks (inside of one unused bigger one) seems to me as if the author intended to write lots of small `unsafe` blocks and accidentally added a large one around them, and it does not seems all that likely that the author intended to write one large `unsafe` blocks and accidentally (perfectly) managed to also enclose every single (smaller set of) unsafe operation(s) in that large `unsafe` block with its own dedicated `unsafe` block. Note that in any case where _not_ all unsafe operations have their own smaller `unsafe` block, the lint will still effectively nudge the user to using a single large `unsafe` block anyways (because there’s no good way to suggest adding extra `unsafe` blocks and also the lint (as implemented in this PR) only goes as far as to point to a containing `unsafe` block, and doesn’t also note where else that containing `unsafe` block is actually being used (although that might actually be a reasonable future addition)). The user would need to remove the outer `unsafe` block themself to find the places where it used to be used (directly).

Certainly, on that last point, the case where only a single inner `unsafe` block appears inside a single outer `unsafe` block is a bit of a special case, because then the edit-distance for removing either the outer or the inner one becomes the same; but special-casing on that seems maybe a bit inconsistent to me.

author: steffahn

body:
FYI, I have also opened a discussion here: https://internals.rust-lang.org/t/unnecessary-unsafe-block/16144, the answers didn't really address the concrete question about the warnings as much, but the answers do AFAICT reflect the situation that different people have different opinions about whether `unsafe` blocks should be larger or smaller, and there's no clear consensus.

Of course this lint will hinder nobody in using unsafe blocks, large or small, anyways, as it only applies if -- for some weird reason -- you end up having *both* a large **and** an exhaustive set of small `unsafe` blocks. I also suspect that the specific case where this decision matters is reached quite rarely anyways, and that it will virtually never lead to someone using a different kind of `unsafe` granularity than they intended to have. Realistic scenarios I can come up with where this case is reached are
* the macro case I already explained (where this change helps in fixing a bug that currently exists in the lint without requiring too complex logic)
* someone trying to make sense of the lint's behavior and playing around with different kinds of test code containing unnecessary unsafe blocks (i.e. people like me when I started deciding to create this PR)
* someone who is currently refactoring from a large to one or multiple small unsafe blocks, or vice versa, and for some reason, (even perhaps automatically through their IDE), ran `cargo check` in the middle of that refactoring (but in this case, they know what they want and the linting behavior will not influence them)

author: steffahn

body:
Dang it, I just lost a previous version of this response due to my desktop environment crashing… let me re-write the main points as far as I remember.

The `hir_id` here can refer to any body owner, not just functions; this includes closures… well actually, closures are ruled-out because they’re handled differently in `fn unsafety_check_result`… and constants, e.g. const items, constants in types, the length in an in array expression, etc. Hence, failure to get a `sig` is not a bug. Failure to `.find()` the id in the first place is already handled by `fn_sig_by_hir_id` internally with a call to `bug!`.

<hr>

Note that an incorrect `Context::Safe` is not actually dangerous. This context is only used for the traversal for `unused_unsafe` warnings. Worst thing that could happen is that an unnecessary unsafe block (e.g. one that’s appearing inside of an `unsafe fn`) is not reported as such.

body:
Actually, we do know it at borrowck time, so we could encode that info in the borrowck result.

But maybe we're going about this the wrong way. When we force mir borrowck and then steal the mir, we own the mir, so we can mutate it and change its tainted field. This is essentially the same idea as the merging of the tainted flags into one query only we're putting it right in the data we have to load anyway

author: bjorn3

body: The personality slot could be split up to store both elements separately. That would save on field projecrions.
author: bjorn3

body: Hm, I think we have to rebuild the slot into one 'value' (at least on LLVM) due to resume taking a single value -- so I'm not sure that's worth it. An array as you suggest in the other comment also involves some weirdness since the contained values aren't the same (or even the same size) -- `{ *mut u8, i32 }` today.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
> An array as you suggest in the other comment also involves some weirdness since the contained values aren't the same (or even the same size) -- { *mut u8, i32 } today.

The array would have `Value` values as elements. So basically you would do `bx.resume(&[lp1, lp2]);` instead of `bx.resume(lp);` and then the codegen backend could combine it into a struct if necessary, or keep it separate. Another option would be to take two separate arguments like so: `bx.resume(lp1, lp2)`.
author: bjorn3

body: Ah, you meant at the internal Rust level -- sure, yes, that seems possible to change. I can try that out.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
Hm -- @bjorn3 my worry is that if we split up the landing pad slot such that it's two separate allocas, then we are largely back to the current situation where LLVM IR is somewhat verbose to unpack the result of a landingpad instruction (which returns the aggregate in LLVM IR) and then re-pack it when invoking resume later. It doesn't seem like it would really carry ~any performance impact, but I'm not sure it's worth making this refactoring given the need to unpack and repack at least with LLVM.

I also took a look at whether we could avoid the unpacking and then repacking by having a more linear control flow (i.e., presuming that each landing pad is eventually followed by a resume, so we can just use the SSA variable result of landingpad instruction directly), but it seems difficult to make that change with today's compiler at least (though I suspect it might be a good idea; I'm not sure it's worth it though).

I think my sense is that we should just close this PR at this point and chalk it up as not really helpful.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
> It doesn't seem like it would really carry ~any performance impact

I wouldn't expect it to given that the resume instruction turns into a tail call to `_Unwind_Resume` with both struct fields as separate arguments in separate registers. The landing pad also gets it passed in using two separate registers.
author: bjorn3

body:
Sure, there's no runtime impact -- this PR is mostly about our codegen of LLVM IR rather than runtime code.

It seems to me that if we make cleanup_landing_pad return `[Self::Value; 2]` that's still going to force us to generate unpacking and repacking code with LLVM, since it's expecting an aggregate rather than distinct functions. It seems like what we almost want is a backend-specific type here, since some backends want it as an aggregate and some don't. But that seems way overkill, which is why I'm increasingly leaning towards doing nothing here.

author: emilyalbini

body:
I agree the format should be extensible, but I think the schema I have achieves most of that goal.

As discussed in other comments, we want to track multiple `x.py` invocations into a single file. But, if we had a top-level object, the object wouldn't be able to store information that differs between invocations, such as the wall clock time of the whole invocation.

In the current schema, the role of the top-level object is in the `RustbuildInvocation` node, and all the metadata would be stored there. Having the top-level array then allows to easily store multiple of those invocations into a single file.

The only metadata I can see us wanting to store in the top level is maybe a format version number, but I'm not sure it's worth changing the schema just for that. What do you think?
author: emilyalbini

body:
At least some consumers -- e.g., total wall time analysis like my rustc-ci-timing scripts -- don't care about individual rustbuild invocations.

I also think in general a top-level object future-proofs us against any expansion we want to do, and in general unless there's a strong motivation to avoid it I'd prefer to have it (even if the only field is a "invocations" array).

As I mentioned elsewhere there are some properties that are more machine-dependent than invocation-specific that I'd like to add in the future (e.g., CPU model, disk space, etc.) that are useful to have on a per-job level IMO. Those are not really well-mapped to an individual x.py invocation. Likewise, I think it is useful to have the ability to represent e.g. CPU time spent *outside* x.py invocations, for example if we start trying to track the duration of AWS uploads or docker builds. Both of those don't map well to "RustbuildInvocations", and while we can have an array of differently-typed objects, that gets somewhat unwieldy fast, IMO.

This sort of interleaving is why I'm a little doubtful about the reset trigger at all, since it doesn't really seem like it's going to produce good results to me.

body:
I kind-of prefer the original sentence here. What’s the motivation for the change? The call to `drain` itself does not do anything (yet) beyond creating an iterator. The returned iterator has a lifetime coupled to the mutable borrow; for an operation that “Removes the specified range … returning all removed elements as an iterator” one could expect an iterator that owns the elements and can be created with a shorter mutable borrow not coupled to a lifetime parameter of the type of the iterator.

The same applies to `Vec` and `String`

author: steffahn

body:
Well, this paragraph kind-of says to me "If you want to, during iteration, change your mind and keep the range inside of the original string, feel free to go ahead and just `mem::forget` the iterator. This is a supported use-case and we guarantee the precise behavior in this case". I.e. it promises a certain behavior on `mem::forget`ting the iterator, and thus (implicitly) ***encourages*** people to deliberately leak the iterator to achieve certain behavior. If we *wanted* to offer an actual documented and guaranteed way of avoiding the draining, then it should be via a (`self`-consuming) method on the iterator, not by giving any promises on "what happens on `mem::forget` IMO.  (E.g. one could create a `fn cancel(self)` on the `string::Drain` iterator that explicitly doesn't remove anything from the `String`, or `vec::Drain` could provide a method that puts back the remaining elements into the original `Vec`.)

All we should do in these kinds of documentation is say, very explicitly, something along the lines of "please ***don't ever*** skip dropping these iterators properly, it is considered a logic error and can, among other things, leave the original structure in an arbitrary unspecified (yet safe-to-use in terms of memory-safety) state with potentially surprising and unexpected behavior when you continue using it afterwards, it can result in arbitrary amounts of additional leaks of the to-be-drained items and/or other items in the same original data structure". I.e. ***strongly discourage*** actually leaking the iterators, and perhaps provide a non-exhaustive list of things that *can* happen upon leaking it anyways, without giving any promises that constrain the set of things that can happen any more than necessary by Rust's memory safety guarantees.

body:
Generally looks great, thanks for working on this!

I mentioned in the review, but I think each portion of the sidebar that has a heading should get its own `<div class="block">`. That would make the HTML structure more consistent, and also simplify the styling. If we want to get fancy, the [`<section>` element](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/section) actually seems like a nice semantic replacement for `<div class="block">`. But I don't feel strongly about whether we use it or not.

By the way, I notice that wherever we use `<div class="block">`, there's a second class, like `block items`, `block struct` etc. Do we ever actually use that second class? Can we get rid of it?
author: jsha

body:
It's usually best to avoid the `:not()` selector. But also, I'm surprised you needed to add this. The existing `.block { margin-bottom: 2em }` rule should have been enough. Looking at the HTML, it looks like the whole first part of the sidebar (items on this page) is contained in a single `.block`. But down below, for items in this module, there's a separate block for each heading. So the structure is like: ```

...

    ...
``` We should make the structure up above the same, so there is a separate block for each heading plus the `
    ` underneath it. Then you won't need the extra CSS rule here, and the format of the HTML will be more consistent.

body:
I think this would be better:

```suggestion
.sidebar-elems li {
```

The rule doesn't need to be specific to `.block.items`. For instance, it should apply to the "items in this module" section too. Also: selecting on `li` instead of `a` is a nice easy way to say "Headings (h2, h3) can wrap, even when they contain an `<a>` since we generated them and we know they are of a reasonable length. However, list items are Rust item names; they're out of our control, sometimes quite long, and wrap weirdly. Truncate them rather than wrap."

body:
Not without replacing it with some extra data, I tried it but wasn't able to do it right away.

The first issue is that rustdoc (unlike rustc) resolves paths differently in inner and outer attributes.
So `item_id` will be the same for inner and outer paths, but their `module_id`s will be different.

There are also may be nuances with how `item_id` and `module_id` are assigned for reexports, but I didn't investigate it.

I agree that ideally we would have an `item_id` and some additional orthogonal flags here instead of the `module_id`.

body:
I think this should add an explicit paragraph about being write-only (along these lines):

> ExitCode is intended to be consumed only by the standard library (via `Termination::report()`), and intentionally does not provide accessors like PartialEq/Eq/Hash/...

This will help in the future if someone asks to add PartialEq, since we can know to be a little more careful, but also to help newcomers to avoid digging in to try to figure out how to access the inner code or compare to SUCCESS, etc. -- this tells them there's nothing hidden, it's intentionally not possible.

author: Kixunil

body:
> I think this may lead people to believe that e.g. SIGINT from Ctrl+C are UB in Rust, which (AFAIK) is not true.

I'm not really sure how best to avoid this misinterpretation. I think part of the issue here is I don't have a clear mental model for exactly what signals should be used for or what they represent. I just did some reading to try to get a better picture, but the best idea I can think of is to reword the last sentence a bit to make it more focused on this specific interface rather than the task it represents. Something like:

> `Termination` uses `ExitCode` instead of `ExitStatus` because `Termination` is meant to represent normal (aka graceful) termination in a cross platform manner, and signals are not supported on all Tier 1 platforms supported by Rust. Additionally, where they are used for process termination, signals are generally used to abruptly kill a process from a separate process running within the same OS, not as a return value from `main`.

It still feels a bit rough, but I'm curious what you think, or if you have a better way to phrase this in mind.

> > It also seems like we _could_ support ending as-if a signal had occurred, right?
> 
> I believe we'd have to switch the handler to default and then self-send signal. Which is not guaranteed to be delivered. Sounds very tricky and scary.

I'm not an expert on signals by any means, but I think it is also the case that signal handling is fairly platform specific. A quick glance at the various ExitStatusExt traits show that extracting signals isn't part of the cross platform interface for `ExitStatus`. It's available on unix via the extension trait and it's not available on windows at all.

Even if we could plausibly introduce an interface for exiting via a signal I suspect that what we would want is closer to `std::process::exit` than it would be to `Termination::report`.
author: yaahc

body:
I think it might help to reframe this as something more positive (rather than focusing on the "lack" of support for signals). Maybe something like:

> `ExitCode` is intended for terminating the currently running process, via the `Termination` trait, in contrast to `ExitStatus`, which represents the termination of a child process. These APIs are separate due to platform compatibility differences and their expected usage; it is not generally possible to exactly reproduce an ExitStatus from a child for the current process after the fact.

This does make me think that maybe naming them ExitCode and ExitStatus is a bit confusing and we should hunt for a different name, but my "best" ideas are ... bad, so I think we shouldn't block stabilization on this. It feels a little weird that we have Termination with `report(self) -> ExitCode` rather than `exit(ExitCode)`, and some From impls, but I guess that ship has somewhat sailed given how late in the process things are and the partial exposure of Termination to stable code -- though I *think* we could get away with a change if main basically returned `impl Into<ExitCode>` which is effectively a less-fancy name for Termination.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
:+1:, I like this framing

> This does make me think that maybe naming them ExitCode and ExitStatus is a bit confusing and we should hunt for a different name, but my "best" ideas are ... bad, so I think we shouldn't block stabilization on this. It feels a little weird that we have Termination with `report(self) -> ExitCode` rather than `exit(ExitCode)`, and some From impls, but I guess that ship has somewhat sailed given how late in the process things are and the partial exposure of Termination to stable code -- though I _think_ we could get away with a change if main basically returned `impl Into<ExitCode>` which is effectively a less-fancy name for Termination.

I don't think `Into` is the appropriate trait here, since the usage of `Termination::report` also heavily implies side effects during the conversion aka printing the error to stderr. Expecting `Into` impls to call `eprintln!` feels like an abuse of the trait. Also, I'm always incredibly worried about running into overlap issues when using the `From`/`Into` traits, so I'm fairly worried about what restrictions we might unknowingly put upon ourselves if we tried to piggyback on existing traits for this.
author: yaahc

body:
> I'm not an expert on signals by any means

I'm not sure if I should call myself "expert" but I did work with them quite a bit and they are **very** ugly interface. Ideally avoid by default. There are some reasonable tricks to use them safely and sanely but self sending doesn't seem that useful. (I did actually see it in practice but can't remember why guys didn't just execute the signal handler directly.)

> Even if we could plausibly introduce an interface for exiting via a signal I suspect that what we would want is closer to `std::process::exit` than it would be to Termination::report`.

Agreed

@Mark-Simulacrum nice description. Can't figure out a better name either.

author: flip1995

body:
I totally agree with you that these not reporting "internal" overflows is the way to go.

The piece that's [still](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/90541#issuecomment-966895088) unclear to me: how is it helpful for something labelled `bigint_helper_methods` for both `MIN + -1` and `MAX + 1` to have the same `true` carry?  It seems to me like it's fundamentally not enough information to "allows for chaining together multiple additions" (like the doc comment says the method can be used to do).

Said otherwise, I can't see a way that it's useful to pass the *output* `bool` back into the *input* `bool` of another call.  Which makes this seem like a less-necessary ternary `overflowing_add` rather than anything that can be used for "carrying".
author: scottmcm

body:
In the example I give in https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/85532#issuecomment-967523098, to implement the `overflowing_add/_sub` operations on bit ints, you first need to perform the add operation on the unsigned low order data first.
When `carrying_add`/`borrowing_sub` to/from the highest order data, which is signed for signed big ints, the input `bool` comes from an unsigned overflow, but the output inform on signed overflow, which is exactly the return value necessary to implement the `overflowing_add/_sub` on bit ints.

To summarize, when implementing bit ints, the workflow is to chain the unsigned overflow from the low order data to the higher order ones, and when performing the last operation, the result is not chained, but in fact used to indicate what happened during the operation.
**In other words, signed overflow will never be chained, because it's not indented to. It's intended to inform on the result of the overall operation.**

In my experience with the Motorola 68000, this is the intended behavior on big ints. The unsigned overflow flag is chained across `ADD` operations to perform the addition on a bit int. During the chaining, the signed overflow flag is not used because it is not valid. It becomes valid after the last `ADD` instruction occurred, because the big int implemented is signed.

I hope this explanation is clear.
author: Stovent

body:
Hmm, maybe it would help the conversation to have `carrying_mul` implemented for signed numbers too?

What would the signature be for that?  If I'm following your comment, it sounds like it would take in an *unsigned* carry, because it could come from `uN::carrying_mul`?  And then what would the outgoing carry be?  Is it just a bit again?  Or would it be signed?

Basically, if the unsigned version is <https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u32.html#method.carrying_mul>
```rust
fn carrying_mul(multiplier: u32, multiplicand: u32, carry: u32) -> (u32 /* product */, u32 /*carry*/)
```
Then it would seem weird to me if the signed version were
```rust
fn carrying_mul(multiplier: i32, multiplicand: i32, carry: u32) -> (i32 /* product */, bool /*carry*/)
```
author: scottmcm

body:
Based on an example I made, the implementations are way different between multiplication and addition/subtraction. I think the signature of `carrying_mul` (and by extension `widening_mul` too) would be
```rs
fn carrying_mul(multiplier: i32, multiplicand: i32, carry: i32) -> (i32 /* product */, i32 /* carry */);
fn widening_mul(multiplier: i32, multiplicand: i32) -> (i32 /* product */, i32 /* carry */);
```
You can see my own implementation of `widening_mul` on a I16 big int here : https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=nightly&mode=release&edition=2021&gist=065c022198027bdba3a3ffc4f90e62e2
It is the same code for both signed and unsigned big ints.

The idea of the implementation is that multiplying two big ints together (for example I16, which is made of an u8 and an i8): `lhs * rhs`  results in computing `(lhs.high + lhs.low) * (rhs.high + rhs.low)`. When we distribute the factors, we get
```
lhs.high * rhs.high + lhs.high * rhs.low + lhs.low * rhs.high + lhs.low * rhs.low
```
What may look strange in signed contexts is the fact that the multiplicand is cast from unsigned to signed before being used, but it is mandatory to use signed semantics to obtain the correct result.

If you want to I can provide a complete breakdown on what happens when running this code on both a signed big int and an unsigned big int.

author: cjgillot

body:
`rustc_privacy` has two private-in-public checkers
- old and very buggy `ObsoleteVisiblePrivateTypesVisitor`
- properly implemented `PrivateItemsInPublicInterfacesVisitor`

The latter one caused too much breakage when originally implemented (https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/34537), so it was decided to report errors from the new checker as warnings when the old checker does not report them.

"Restricted" visibilities (`pub(...)`) were unstable back then, so we could break code using them, so the new checker was enabled for such code without any `ObsoleteVisiblePrivateTypesVisitor`-based compatibility measures.
author: petrochenkov

All these hacks will become obsolete once https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/48054 is implemented.

For now I suggest to just evaluate the `has_pub_restricted` during ~AST lowering~ late resolution and put in into ~HIR~ resolver outputs.

body:
We specifically need to deny this:
```rust
impl Trait<Self::A> for () {}
```

But we should not deny this:
```rust
impl Trait<Self> for () {}
```

So we use this flag during astconv to catch cases (like the former) where we have an associated type with a `Self` in the path, which without this flag would try to elaborate the supertraits of the trait ref, looking for one with the associated type. 

Since resolving the trait ref would itself require resolving an associated type in the trait ref, we get a cycle error.

body:
> the public method now simply forwards to the internal method

This is not accurate if `_from_vec_unchecked` already got inlined into `from_vec_unchecked`. In that situation `inline(always)` would be forcing a potentially much larger chunk of code than intended to be duplicated into every call site.

We tend to be skeptical of `inline(always)` without compelling benchmarks that demonstrate it's the only way to achieve certain commonly-necessary optimizations downstream.

You're making it sound like `_from_vec_unchecked` could be inlined into `from_vec_unchecked` and then all of that gets inlined into the call site.

body:
It feels like the added bit here that might be good is to contrast this with IntervalSet; intuitively (but tell me if you think I'm wrong):

* ChunkedBitSet is good with *many* long stretches of zeroes or ones (e.g., domain in the tens of millions of points, hundreds of thousands of intervals each spanning several thousand points).
   * Forces *at least* (total domain) / (2048) * 24 bytes (`size_of::<Chunk>`) allocation -- for huge domain, that can already be pretty big.
* IntervalSet is optimized for arbitrarily large domain or interval size, particularly if you expect *few* intervals
   * In particular, no problem if the intervals are not distributed throughout the domain nicely.


As I write the above out, it's increasingly feeling like this adds (over IntervalSet) basically:

* Constant-time access (vs. O(log number of intervals)) to points

at the cost of:

* Up-front allocation for entire domain


Maybe worth including some parts of this in the docs -- mostly just laying out thoughts in case they seem helpful.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
Comment on why an Rc here? At first glance, probably not obvious.

Also, why `Rc<[Word]>` -- shouldn't we know the chunk width upfront, and this can be `Rc<[Word; CHUNK_WORDS]>`? That'll save space since we skip storing the length.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body: For the second question: we know the chunk width for all chunks except the final one, which can be any length in the range `1..=CHUNK_WORDS`. And lots of sets are small, and so only have one chunk.
author: nnethercote

body: I tried doing `Rc<Word; CHUNK_WORDS>` and it ends up being better. Good suggestion :)
author: nnethercote

body:
This seems likely to incur several copies of the (not so small) array -- maybe worth trying to optimize a little and allocate into the Rc first thing? We could even consider [`Rc::new_zeroed`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/std/rc/struct.Rc.html#method.new_zeroed) as an extra win for being able to calloc the memory which could avoid a zeroing pass entirely in some cases.

(After the memory is allocated, Rc::get_mut().unwrap() should work in here).
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
Why are we storing the domain size in each chunk in the first place? It might be better to store in the top-level ChunkedBitSet a domain size for the last chunk, and branch on that?

I guess we're unlikely to save much memory if we insist on storing a count with the Mixed variant -- but maybe we'd end up with 10 bytes vs. 16? It might not be that important.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
FWIW, my suspicion is that this will not optimize well -- it might be better to do something like `vec![Chunk::new(...); num_chunks].into_boxed_slice()` and then update the last chunk in-place.

Ultimately initialization is probably not the most important thing, though.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
It seems like if we're going to do this, then we might as well check up-front before we copy all the memory over?

FWIW, if the 'last' bit is frequently getting set and cleared, this is going to be pretty terrible for performance. I'm wondering if we should actually not have dedicated Ones and Zeroes variants, and instead have a *global* Rc for each that we can clone a bunch -- not sure if it's actually worth it, but maybe worth considering. (The unfortunate reality is that Rc::new is not const, so the global would need to be a LazyOnceCell today -- that might make it not cost effective).
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
nit: it's probably better to initialize with `[!0; CHUNK_WORDS]` -- or we might borrow from my earlier comment about initializing in-place inside the Rc.

In a toy example at least I'm seeing the zeroing and a subsequent fill look pretty unfortunate in terms of generated assembly, particularly when we're going to end up clearing the excess bits regardless.

Not sure how much it'll matter in practice.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
Having the domain size in each chunk is *very* convenient, it's used all the time, and avoids the need for lots of "is this the last chunk?" tests.

And I don't think it would reduce the size because the `Rc` still needs word alignment, so we'd just end up with more padding.
author: nnethercote

body: Yeah, it doesn't matter for perf but your suggestion makes the code easier to read.
author: nnethercote

body: One copy, yes, but not several? I originally allocated the `Rc` first and did the `get_mut().unwrap()` thing and I thought it was ugly and switched to doing it this way, which didn't affect perf :)  I didn't know about `Rc::new_zeroed`, though.
author: nnethercote

body: I guess LLVM sees through it -- I could easily imagine the big array getting copied more than once before it arrives at the memory allocation across the stack and Rc::new. Seems OK if perf is OK, though.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
The point about the check is good, I have addressed that in `insert` and `remove`.

A global Rc makes sense for Zeros because it's always all zeros. But Ones chunks can have trailing zeroes (and several operations rely on these) so you'd only be able to have a global Rc for the case where the ones fill all the way to the end.

But I don't think the global Rc would help anyway? As soon as you move away from all zeroes or all ones you have to duplicate anyway.

This doesn't work for the `Vec` test case.

<details>

```
error[E0412]: cannot find type `T` in this scope
 --> src/test/ui/functions-closures/fn-help-with-err-generic-is-not-function.rs:2:13
  |
2 | impl Struct<T>
  |     -       ^ not found in this scope
  |     |
  |     help: you might be missing a type parameter: `<T>`

error[E0412]: cannot find type `T` in this scope
 --> src/test/ui/functions-closures/fn-help-with-err-generic-is-not-function.rs:7:5
  |
7 |     T: Copy,
  |     ^ not found in this scope

error[E0282]: type annotations needed
  --> src/test/ui/functions-closures/fn-help-with-err-generic-is-not-function.rs:14:31
   |
14 |     fn method(v: Vec<u8>) { v.len(); }
   |                               ^^^ cannot infer type
   |
   = note: type must be known at this point

 INFO rustc_typeck::check::method::suggest check deref std::vec::Vec<u8> impl FnOnce
error[E0599]: no method named `len` found for struct `Vec<u8>` in the current scope
  --> src/test/ui/functions-closures/fn-help-with-err-generic-is-not-function.rs:14:31
   |
14 |     fn method(v: Vec<u8>) { v.len(); }
   |                               ^^^ private field, not a method
   |
   = note: `v` is a function, perhaps you wish to call it

┐rustc_typeck::check::coercion::coerce a=(), b=()
┘
```

</details>

It looks like, based on the added trace I put in here, the `Vec<u8>` itself returns true from `predicate_may_hold`. It derefs to an error type, so if we check the deref chain completely we can avoid this bug, but by doing it this way, we cause it to short-circuit at the first matching type.

body:
One issue I'm encountering. I don't think there's a sound way to determine what the appropriate `Output` type should be. The function `check_overloaded_binop` attempts to do this, but it can only compute `return_ty` if the initial `lookup_op_method` returns a `MethodCallee`, which it does not in the case being addressed. 

Is there some possibility here that I'm missing?
author: willcrichton

body: I'm hoping that the correct associated type bound is in the `errors` list. Try dumping the entire list and checking if it contains `PredicateKind::Projection` that match the `pred` in the loop here.
author: oli-obk

body: Yes I've checked, and a `Projection` predicate does not already exist. This is because the relevant predicates are generated by the function [`obligation_for_method`](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/master/compiler/rustc_typeck/src/check/method/mod.rs#L298). That function would have to be extended to produce a `Projection` predicate, except it can only do so if it knows about the expected associated types of a method, which it cannot in this case.

> Which obligation code was previously used for this case?

That's the problem: almost any obligation code could have been previously used for this case. This code is used in any case where a function call introduces an obligation, but the obligation cannot be tracked down to a particular function parameter, so the function callee itself is used for the span.

> Can this new code make any observable changes in tests?

It doesn't, no.

body:
Afaict the issue here is that `Debug` for `Unevaluated` uses `Debug` for `DefId` which uses `def_path_debug_str` which prints the `stable_crate_id`. This ID is presumably not stable between targets so different CI runners disagree about what that ID should be.

It seems like - before this PR - all `Unevaluated` constants only referred to the currently compiled crate. The `stabile_crate_id` of which is probably stable for different targets.

Writing a potential fix rn.
author: lcnr

body:
> This ID is presumably not stable between targets so different CI runners disagree about what that ID should be.

Indeed. Liballoc is compiled by cargo which afaik hashes the target into the value passed in using `-Cmetadata`. The `-Cmetadata` argumemts are one of the inputs to the stable crate id calculation.

author: mikebenfield

body:
How is the number of variants relevant?

It doesn't matter what our *current* optimizations happen to do, the question is whether the MIR program at hand has well-defined behavior.

Generally, if anything in Miri mentions any optimizations, something is wrong. Miri implements the MIR semantics. Those are not defined by optimizations, it is the other way around -- optimizations are constrained by the MIR semantics.
author: RalfJung
RalfJung

`AddNicheCases` will not apply if there are only two variants, because LLVM will do a better job of optimizing that case anyway.

As an alternative, I think we *might* be able to modify the `ObligationCauseCode` that gets created to begin with to include either the spans or maybe even better the involved expressions. body:
> It allows to have two different formatting depending on what you need like that.

The issue is that it is entirely unclear which style is used where -- both debug and display end up being user-visible in various situations, and when changing the code it becomes extremely hard to predict which one will change which output. IOW we can't actually meaningfully make them different, currently, since the consequences are so unclear. That is why I entirely *removed* many of the `Display` implementations for Miri types -- then at least there is no ambiguity over which kind of formatting is used.

This PR adds back some of those instances which I removed; if that cannot be avoided I won't block it but then we can at least consistently use the pattern of forwarding one to the other.

author: CAD97

body:
This is, of course, the hard part of this PR.

I'm thinking of the guarantee in a similar way to that of references, in that it's a "while the handle exists." Of course, under the Stacked Borrows model (and, to an extent, NLL), it's _not_ when the borrow exists, but until when the borrow is last used.

So there're two ways I currently see to explain the weaker (not pin safe) guarantee, borrowing language from references:

- The memory regions are valid for the lifetime of the allocator. This is complicated by the written `'static` lifetime not actually being "the lifetime of the allocator" in this fashion, but an upper bound, so probably not a good way to explain it.
- The memory regions are valid until the point when the last clone is last used.

Effectively, what we're looking for is the dual of the pinning guarantee.

So I _think_ how I'd draft this is

> Memory blocks must retain their validity until the allocator instance and all of its clones are dropped, forgotten, leaked, or otherwise never accessed again. (In other words, when a lifetime held by the allocator instance is allowed to expire.)

(And now writing that I'm interested in how the `#[may_dangle]` borrowck eyepatch interacts with the guarantee, since that (very roughly) allows a lifetime to expire before the container. IIUC it doesn't, since the eyepatch refers to the lifetime within a generic argument, but I'm never certain w.r.t. may_dangle.)
author: CAD97

body: I updated the wording to be more explicit about these conditions. Does that work?
author: djkoloski

body: I don't think you need both of these bounds, just the `~const PinSafeAllocator` one. `A: ~const PinSafeAllocator` implies `A: PinSafeAllocator`.
author: compiler-errors

body: Ah, you're right. I'm new to the `~const Trait` syntax. This is fixed now.
author: djkoloski

body: Do you mind changing this to `A: ~const PinSafeAllocator` everywhere else too?

author: lcnr

body:
We might even consider going to

```suggestion
pub fn from_fn<T, const N: usize>(mut cb: impl FnMut(usize) -> T) -> [T; N]
```

Because if the rules change to "you can turbofish the explicit generic parameters but not the APITs", then that would be nicer -- `from_fn::<i32, N>(...)` instead of `::<i32, N, _>`.

That would, of course, mean that they'd need to be provided by context in the mean time, however.  But TBH I prefer that anyway, as I find `from_fn::<_, 4, _>` distasteful.  (The closure almost always provides the `T`.)

body: Ah lol, yea, inlining cost boundaries are fun. Yea, just slap the attribute on all previously generic methods, that should make callers big enough again to stop them from being inlined. Alternatively add `inline(never)` with a comment to functions that disappeared from cachegrind.
author: oli-obk

body:
That's not practical -- almost *every single* method became non-generic after removing the parameter; that's the whole reason for the big win on compile times. IMO trying to second-guess LLVM here on inlining (in either direction) is not the right approach, we're not likely to find a magic bullet.

The diff above is illustrative of typical inlining decisions slightly changing, but trying to recover from that by adding attributes isn't likely to really help that much -- the changes are too scattered and ultimately I suspect arise from things that we can't really influence easily.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
I thought the gains are from not instantiating the generics in 5 different crates and generating LLVM IR for them and optimizing them?

Having them only get compiled once is a different aspect than removing the implicit `#[inline]` that generic function have
author: oli-obk

body: `#[inline]` on all of these methods will -- AFAIK -- force generation in every crate that uses them; it's equivalent to generics in that respect. It also adds an inline hint, but that's a separate effect and unlikely to matter as much given the large size of many of the functions here. We save a little of course on generating just one copy per crate instead of 2-3, but that's still a higher cost.

body:
Yeah it requires knowledge that the only two enum variants are `Scalar` and `Uninit`. `let else` hides all non matching patterns into a single `else` clause. This also has maintainability issues as you won't get an non exhaustive match error if you add a variant that might be relevant for the code. I have added variants in the past and the non exhaustive match error was very helpful because it pointed me to all those places, some you might never have thought of, that needed change. Generally this second issue exists with `if let` as well though.

Anyways, I'll change it back.

I think it's reasonable to use `let else` for enums where it's reasonably obvious that there are only two cases. If you have a variant `Async::Yes` and the only other variant is `Async::No`, you don't have to spell out the latter to guess (correctly) that only those two variants exist.

(By contrast, in the `ScalarMaybeUninit` case above, it's a little less obvious. Though honestly I wonder if that case would be clearer as an `Option` newtype.)

body: I think we lose some clarity when moving to let/else over an exhaustive match, in pretty much all cases. For example, Async::No is *probably* the only other variant, but even so, when reading the above code I'm sort of left to wonder "is that the other variant" and re-parse it out such that the yes case is here and the no case is there. Plus, with a slightly complicated pattern on the Yes variant, it's possible for *some* of the Yes's to also be put into the No variant (e.g., if it was something like `Async::Yes(Some(foo), ..)`, and so I end up spending more time parsing the pattern when the match would've been immediately clear.

Like, if the scalar has `Multiple` layout, then that one scalar contains its discriminant and hence we can definitely set `NoUndef`? (This might miss newtypes around such enums, not sure if that's a problem.)

You're right.

I'm a bit hesistant to add more complex logic here. I think the missing cases are fairly rare, just `Option<NonZero*/NonNull>` (and equivalent types), and enums with exactly 256 (or 65536, etc.) variants. In the long run we can switch to the `uninit` tracking added by #94527 and handle these cases without having to inspect the shape of the layout at all.

author: nikic

body: This doesn't look right to me. Why would the offset of the second pair element be relevant for the alignment of the value it points to?
author: nikic

body:
Both elements of a scalar pair share the same `TyAndLayout`, the offset is used by `layout.pointee_info_at` to determine which element of the pair is being loaded.

This is ~the same logic as is used to generate `align` attributes for scalar pair arguments--
`adjust_for_rust_scalar` calls `pointee_info_at` here: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/9fbff89354ba7a4ec2d1191a3f405cbe408370f6/compiler/rustc_middle/src/ty/layout.rs#L3070
...which is called from the closure passed to `ArgAbi::new`: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/9fbff89354ba7a4ec2d1191a3f405cbe408370f6/compiler/rustc_middle/src/ty/layout.rs#L3135
...which is in turn called for both parts of a scalar pair (with the same layout and different offsets) here: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/9556d7a09a7e80e84430071b29d118f39dde8205/compiler/rustc_target/src/abi/call/mod.rs#L458-L461

body: Thanks, you're entirely correct.

body:
I don't understand why it wouldn't be correct, if no `--check-cfg values(...)` are provided no values checking is going to be performed. Representing this state could be done with an empty `FxHashMap` but I changed to an `Option<FxHashMap<...>>` for these reasons:
 - Consistency with `names_valid`.
 - Easier to understand with an `Option` the nullity/emptyness of `values_valid` and by extension it's non-checking.
 - It's also probably faster to check `None` than trying to get a element from an empty `FxHashMap`.

But if you disagree I can still change it back to just: `FxHashMap<T, FxHashSet<T>>`.

body:
The warning message is not meant to explain the whole issue. Just point out in a one-liner (optimally) what's wrong with the code. For more details there is the lint documentation.

A good rule of thumb for good error messages is the rule on how error messages should look like:
- start lower case
- don't have any punctuation (at the end)

In this case a good message would be something along the lines of: 

```
"temporary with significant drop glue in match scrutinee"
```

And then in a `note` you can add `"the lifetime of this temporary gets extended to the end of the match expression"`.

Feel free to improve the wording.

Take a look at `span_lint_and_then` in the rest of Clippy on how to contruct more complex error messages and suggestions.

body:
So after the discussion on Zulip getting the span with `expr.shrink_to_lo()` and then suggesting to insert `let value = ...;\n` may just work. So:

```suggestion
                            (expr.span.shrink_to_lo(), replacement.to_string()),
                            (expr_span, "value".to_owned()),
```

and removing the "match" from the `replacement` string.

With that you also won't have to do the whole `reindent_multiline` thing.

Wait what? `value` is always just `()`, because an assignment has the type `()` AFAIK. 

So this makes me question, if we need special casing for this kind of `ExprKind` at all. I don't expect to see code like this much in the real world. How would the suggestion look like, if you don't add special casing for `Assign[Op]` expressions? I expect it would move the `mutex.lock().unwrap().i` up and then it wouldn't get assigned anymore.

So while we're at special casing this, why not go further and replace `value` in that case with `()` and don't use `let value = ` in the "moving-out" suggestion at all? But maybe that's a step too far :thinking:

body: This is intended to test a heuristic that does not place a stack protector for arrays (strictly) smaller than 8, so it should be using size 7. The test still worked previously, because the `[8 x i8]` alloca was converted into an `i64` alloca, and did not receive the stack protector for that reason. This doesn't happen anymore with opaque pointers. The way these heuristics are currently implemented (inspecting alloca types in the backend) is unfortunately not stable under optimization (probably it should be a frontend responsibility to add metadata to allocas that should receive a protector, or something like that).

body:
There's three issues here:
* `Abi::ScalarPair` should not be special-cased in call ABIs - it's just `Abi::Aggregate` with extra *Rust-only* optimizations tacked on (none of the other issues matter if the `parse_structure` codepath is taken)
* this is not how one computes the offset of the second scalar for an `Abi::ScalarPair` (`scalar1.size(cx).align_to(scalar2.align(cx).abi)` is more or less the typical formula, anything else risks being wrong for certain scalars)
* `else if scalar2.value == abi::F64` is probably a typo? certainly seems like the intention is to get the size of `scalar1` (which is just `scalar1.size(cx)`)

As it stands, I believe this code passes `(u128, f64)` incorrectly (thanks to alignment, I can't think of anything else that would be mismatched), so it was probably unobserved for that reason.
author: eddyb

body:
This is a silent failure (it will cause data corruption AFAICT). If the fields aren't in ascending order, this is a non-C-friendly type and you have to either ICE (which is arguably unfriendly), or better just fall back to "this cannot be passed in any optimized way".

Sadly SPARC64 seems to be one of the architectures unfortunate enough to use "turn `struct` into as many immediate arguments as needed", instead of `byval`, for on-stack passing.

But still, you could use something similar to the `Union` case handled a bit above.

Actually, wait, this is wronger than I thought, it doesn't add field offsets to the enclosing `struct`'s offset, just replaces it altogether.

There's no way the testcases added in this PR could work, I think it's just hitting all the silent failure modes around here and `last_offset` and accidentally causing the right output...

body:
I think this is a good idea, but this will cause a behavior change. Currently, using an unstable lint results in a hard error by default. This change would make using an unstable lint into a warning by default because the `unknown_lints` lint is set to `Warn` by default.

I put this together locally; here's what the output would look like:

`-Wunknown_lints` (default)

```
warning[E0658]: the `test_unstable_lint` lint is unstable
   |
   = help: add `#![feature(test_unstable_lint)]` to the crate attributes to enable
   = note: this is ignored but not enabled by the `unknown_lints` lint
```

`-Dunknown_lints`

```
error[E0658]: the `test_unstable_lint` lint is unstable
  |
  = help: add `#![feature(test_unstable_lint)]` to the crate attributes to enable
```

`-Aunknown_lints` ignores the lint and produces no output.

The downstream impact of this change would be that the `non_exhaustive_omitted_patterns_lint` and `must_not_suspend` lints will change behavior from producing an error by default, to producing a warning by default.

body:
We're missing probably the largest chunk of things, and that is calling generic functions that clone their generic parameters. MIR opts will not work there, as they only see the generic function, and the `Clone` bounds don't allow switching to `Copy` in that case. This is a recurring problem with other MIR opts, too.

That said, the effects on a real world benchmark are neat though I do wonder how much of that is these benchmarks being old and not having had clippy applied to them (which will tell you to do this transformation in user space, and even on generic things).
author: oli-obk

body: This should probably live next to `is_trivially_sized` and should probably be used to speed up `is_copy_modulo_regions` by avoiding a query call.

body:
It might be a good idea to document this in the tracking issue for this feature, and consider if there's some kind of crate-level opt-in that can be added -- I suspect in many cases folks will want to have this sort of crate-private cfg without end users needing to opt-in to it.

It's also the case that the proc-macro2 library for example I think **intends** for this to be used by users, but doesn't expose it as a Cargo feature to avoid accidental use (e.g., by a library that enables that feature), forcing the 'last' user to actually pass the relevant cfg.

I think this is fine for this PR but we should consider this as part of the feature development process, especially if and when stabilization is considered.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
It's probably an issue if stable hashes differ between incremental and non-incremental mode. I can imagine that, for example, it leads to linker errors if an upstream crate is compiled in non-incremental mode and then a down-stream crate is compiled in incremental mode, because the downstream crate computes a different symbol hash.

Caching the hash inside of a std::lazy::once (or something similar) might be worth looking into.

author: JakobDegen

body:
This probably needs a comment...

---

Typically, the value produced by an expression is consumed by its parent in some way, so we only have to check if the parent contains a yield (note that the parent may, for example, store the value into a local variable, but then we already consider local variables to be live across their scope). 

However, in the case of temporary values, we are going to store the value into a temporary on the stack that is live for the current temporary scope and then return return a reference to it. That value may be live across the entire temporary scope.

body: I'm not sure how that flag would work. Typeck runs on an inferctxt that is not spawned by the trait engine. That inferctxt invokes the trait engine and has done decisions on the flag before it ever sees the engine. We need to keep these decisions unresolved until we actually enter the trait engine, so I don't see a way around that. The alternative would be to do all of typeck twice, but that would make the "diff the errors" much harder.

body:
The proper way to get this path would be to use `builder.llvm_out(builder.config.build).join("lib")`. However, even though this is the proper directory where LLVM is installed, when LLVM is finally packaged into the `stageN/lib` directory, it is actually being copied from `llvm/build/lib` (the build directory) and not from `llvm/lib` (the install directory).

The packaging code uses `llvm-config` to get the path of the LLVM lib to copy. The `llvm-config` is taken from the `llvm/build/bin` directory because of [this](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/master/src/bootstrap/native.rs#L79) line. @Mark-Simulacrum how should we solve this? Duplicate the `!msvc || is_ninja` check here? Or use `prebuilt_llvm_config` immediately after we build LLVM to get the path of the LLVM lib to instrument?
