body:
In a bare-metal CHERI world, programs start out with an all-powerful capability (e.g., at hardware reset the default data capability (DDC) and program counter capability (PCC) are initialized to such values). It's up to the language runtime's startup code to subset that into appropriate capabilities for users of C/Rust/etc. For example in CheriBSD, we chop the root capability into a number of sub-capabilities in `cheri_init_capabilities` which is called from `_start` (https://github.com/CTSRD-CHERI/cheribsd/blob/2681a143af26941d54997cfd47e645dcdfbdf3ea/sys/arm64/cheri/cheri_machdep.c#L49). I'd expect a Rust environment targeting bare metal to expose similar capabilities to create pointers for MMIO access and the like. How to expose those capabilities will surely require some exploration.

P.S. The split linked above is less complete that ideal (e.g., the kernel root overlaps with user space), but the split allows us to express our intent and should eventually let us tighten things down further.

body:
It will only need quoting if it contains a `"`, which cannot occur in Windows file names. The problem with `append_arg` is it requires a `OsString` whereas at this point we have the UTF-16 string that was used to test if the file exists. I admit I'm somewhat reluctant to keep switching between encodings.

For now maybe an alternative is to return an error if the script name does in fact contain `"`?
author: ChrisDenton

body:
Can `"` occur in filenames that get interpreted by a driver (e.g. a filesystem driver for a foreign filesystem)?

Could a `\` ever occur at the *end* of a filename that could be executable? Is it theoretically possible to achieve a filename ending in `\` using reparse points, or NTFS Streams, or special `\\` paths? If so, would the `\` escape the `"`?
author: joshtriplett

body: Thank you, this does seem safer.
author: joshtriplett

body:
In [Naming Files, Paths, and Namespaces](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/fileio/naming-a-file) search for "reserved characters". There's a list of characters that are reserved for special use by the OS. These cannot be used in filenames at all. Indeed, `argv[0]` depends upon this fact as a [`"` cannot be escaped in `argv[0]`](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/4ce257ff198d23bdf14e956fbf2fe0fed297201f/library/std/src/sys/windows/args.rs#L79).

A trailing `\` is guarded against [earlier](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/4ce257ff198d23bdf14e956fbf2fe0fed297201f/library/std/src/sys/windows/process.rs#L375) and I cannot think of a way that such a filename could possibly be returned from the OS. Though I guess double checking wouldn't hurt.
author: ChrisDenton

author: petrochenkov

body:
Maybe this enum should have some different name.
It should be short because it's used often.

There are two path resolution modes:
- Speculative mode - we only return the result, but do not report errors or lints, and do not do recovery
- Non-speculative mode - we report errors, do recovery when possible, also report lints, and mark path targets as used (the `record_used` naming comes from this). That's why need some extra data like a node id for linting or a span in this mode.

Maybe call it `enum Finalize`?
It's two letters shorter, not as specific as the old "record used" naming, and we use "finalize" elsewhere in resolve for similar functionality, like `fn finalize_(imports,macro_resolutions)`.

body:
Whoa I had no idea that was allowed. But it doesn't seem equivalent to me. When testing it out, `<() as A>::foo` still requires that `T: Sub` even though the impl has the less strict `T: Super` (you can even leave out the bound entirely on the impl and it still requires that `T: Sub`). [playground](https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2021&gist=24f05ef3968676a7b6b0b30834bf6c65)

By your analogy, I take it that the non-const default impl is the more restrictive `T: Sub`, and the const specializing impl is the more permissive `T: Super`. But if the analogy holds, then I'd expect us to disallow the use of the const specializing impl in a const context.

Allowing bottom left and allowing top right feel the same to me because both of them are allowing you to do something with some of the impls but not with the others.
author: BGR360

body:
The fact that we ignore the weaker bounds on the impl is something we want to change. This is https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/100706 which currently wants to require an explicit `#[refine]` attribute to use the less strict associated items.

yeah, this table is the behavior i expected:

|| General impl, not const  | General impl, const |
|---| ------------- | ------------- |
| More specific impl, not const | Currently allowed  | should be disallowed  |
| More specific impl, const  | should be allowed  | should be allowed |

You could think of `const` functions as having the `trait NonConst: Const {}` as a bound. A function which is not const has more requirements for the caller (as they are not allowed to use this function in a const context).

The intent is not "doing something with some of the impls but not with the others". The intent is "loosening the restrictions on assoc items in specialized impls is ok". If you have some specialized impl, that impl should be usable in all ways the default impl can be used, and maybe more (by loosening a restriction). You should not loose capabilities by going from a default to a specialized impl.

does that explanation make sense to you? it feels somewhat difficult to explain :grin::sweat_smile:

body: The `io::copy` optimizations rely on specialization, currently we're usually not documenting those since specialization itself isn't stable and so may change in the future. At a minimum there should be a caveat in there that it's only current/not guaranteed behavior.
author: the8472

body:
I see. And even beyond Rust not necessarily committing to this there are a bunch of very complicated conditions around whether `copy_file_range` will actually work or whether it needs a fallback.

I was trying here to follow the direction of <https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/io/index.html#platform-specific-behavior>: 

> Many I/O functions throughout the standard library are documented to indicate what various library or syscalls they are delegated to. This is done to help applications both understand what’s happening under the hood as well as investigate any possibly unclear semantics. Note, however, that this is informative, not a binding contract. The implementation of many of these functions are subject to change over time and may call fewer or more syscalls/library functions.

In particular, I was looking at the documentation and then the source to understand if the stdlib would even _attempt_ to use the handoff syscalls or whether there would be any benefit to trying it myself.

How about if I add to the comments

> The use of these syscalls is not guaranteed and may change in future releases.

?
author: sourcefrog

body:

You're correct that it incorrectly fails here.

author: CAD97

body:
I don't know; it would probably depend greatly on what information is available at the callsites.  (Whether the `self.size` or the `n` is more likely to be constant.)

Hmm, this might be too cute, but what about:
```rust
let n = (self.size << 1).checked_mul(n).ok_or(LayoutError)? >> 1;
```
along with a whole bunch of tests, of course.

(The shift can't overflow because `self.size` always has the high bit zero, so the unsigned overflow stops at the right place, and the shift back down at the end just shifts out a low-bit zero to fix the scale.)

author: CAD97

body:
Godbolt comparison: https://rust.godbolt.org/z/a3sb3v3sK

Observation putting this together: we don't have to check `isize:MAX` here; `from_size_align` will of course now do that for us, meaning the implementation *does not change* from `HEAD`, since it was using the safe `from_size_align` already.

The size check when nothing is known is fairly clearly the worst option of the correct ones (3 jumps vs 1). Strangely enough, it looks like doing the check that way is potentially *better* than the `isize::checked_mul` (1 jump vs 2); based on staring at Godbolt it looks like this is because with the `usize` mul we get an overflow check error return and handle `from_size_align` with `cmov`s, whereas with the `isize` mul we need to insert a check and bail for an input high bit first, then do the same thing.

I think reverting this to HEAD beats out the bit tricks by obviousness of implementation alone, given the minimal output difference, but it also looks to me like the bit twiddling impl ends up executing all the same assembly instructions but with an additional two to shl and shr, such that an overflow occurs and returns sooner. If anything, this nano-optimizes for the overflowing case, which we expect to be the less likely case anyway.

Hmm.

So I don't like the syntactic comparison for equality that is throughout this code -- I rather prefer the older code, and I would rather express the correctness conditions logically. Certainly in a-mir-formality that is what I expect us to do. (Something like: for all instances of the struct, the drop impl conditions are met.)

That said, the code is cleaner this way, and the only case I could come up with where using syntactic checks would be a problem seems to [already be rejected](https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2021&gist=227432640a3a031c45fa2304a6889333). I consider that a bug, but it's pre-existing and more of a problem with the other drop code.

```rust
struct Foo<T>
where
    T: Iterator,
    T::Item: Send,
{
    t: T,
}

impl<T, I> Drop for Foo<T>
where
    T: Iterator<Item = I>,
    I: Send,
{
    fn drop(&mut self) { }
}

fn main() { }
```

**Do we have a test like this one? If not, can we add one? Otherwise, r=me.**

body:
Now, since I've come into contact with cross-crate documentation issues (#95717), I think this code path (`DocumentationNotBuilt`) is reached when someone implements a trait from an external crate whose documentation wasn't built. I don't know if that ever happens with `cargo doc` but it sure happens with plain `rustdoc`:

Let there be crate `a` defining `pub trait Tr { fn f(); }` and crate `b` depending on `a` containing `pub struct Ty; impl a::Tr for Ty { fn f() {} }`.
Now execute

```sh
rustc a.rs --crate-type=lib --crate-name=a --edition=2021
rustdoc b.rs --crate-type=lib --crate-name=b --edition=2021 --extern=a=liba.rlib
```

In such case, the `f` in the documentation of `impl a::Tr for Ty` (in `doc/b/struct.Ty.html`) does **not** have an `href` attribute since the link would otherwise be broken. And this is the code region responsible for that.

body:
I think leaving evaluation order indeterminate is a bad idea. We should specify some order eventually, and probably it should be left-to-right. (That's what Miri does.)

The disjointness thing can be enforced without leaving the order indeterminate, using the aliasing model instead. That's better because it can be checked by Miri.
author: RalfJung

body:
I don't think CFG paths are relevant. What's relevant is the actual sequence of statements that are executed when the function runs. There's basically a boolean flag dead-or-alive for each local that starts out 'dead' for locals with annotations, and that is set appropriately by these annotations. Accessing (evaluating the place expression) of a local is UB if it is currently 'dead'.

Actually it's more than that, each StorageLive allows the local to be put at a different location in memory.

body: That's what Miri does, and I think it is the right model.

Come to think of it, we should probably mention that we often don't want to "remove" UB in MIR optimizations in general, since keeping it visible to the codegen backend is important.

body:
`crate::Type::AssocItem` now takes the set of traits in scope at the point where it's written, and not elsewhere (in the crate root module). This is the change that fixes https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/78696.

So we no longer need to collect traits in scope at arbitrary root modules.

(The old buggy behavior was caused by replacing `module_id` with `CRATE_DEF_ID` too early.)

body:
I am on board with this API. Next steps are:

- I think this is the kind of method that would benefit from a usage example in the rustdoc.
- Needs a tracking issue.

Some considerations to note in the tracking issue for discussion before we could stabilize:

- Just change the not-yet-stable `Drop for DrainFilter` to keep rest?
  - Advantage: usually what you want (??)
    - I don't have a good picture what the use cases involve drain_filter and *not* exhausting the iterator obtained from it.
  - Disadvantage: inconsistent with stable `Drop for Drain`
  - If you want to remove rest (matching the current unstable behavior of drain_filter) then you'd need to write `.foreach(drop)` to explicitly drop all the rest of the range that matches the filter.
- `&mut self` instead of `self`?
  - If you're holding a `Drain` inside a struct and are operating on it from a `&mut self` method of the struct, `keep_rest(self)` is impossible to use. :(
    - You'd want something like `mem::replace(&mut self.drain_filter, Vec::new().drain(..)).keep_rest()` but the borrow checker won't like that.
    - Failing that, you'd need to change your `Drain` field to `Option<Drain>` and use `self.drain_filter.take().unwrap().keep_rest()` along with `unwrap()` everywhere else that the drain is used. Not good.
  - We'd need to define behavior of calling `.next()` *after* `.keep_rest()`. Presumably one of:
    - `.next()` returns `None`
    - this is considered incorrect usage and so `.next()` panicks
    - `.keep_rest()` sets a flag inside the Drain which Drop will use to decide its behavior, and you're free to continue accessing iterator elements in between.

body: FWIW I think the way to avoid making equality complicated is to ensure there is a normal form: *all* (parts of( constants of type `&str` (for example) must be a `SliceOrStr`, they can *never* be a lot of `Leaf`s. That ensures that when comparing valtrees of equal type, we never have ambiguity about whether something should be represented as `Leaf` or `SliceOrStr`.

"dereferencing a place" does not make a lot of sense as a concept, and the code doesn't even call `deref_operand`. So something seems odd here.

Is this supposed to be short for something like
```
self.deref_operand(&self.read_immediate(&src.into())?.into())?
```
? I don't think we need a dedicated function for that, it seems short enough -- and the act of reading the immediate (i.e. the ptr) from the place should rather be left explicit, IMO.

Both `ImmTy` and `MPlaceTy` can be converted to `OpTy` via `into`.
author: RalfJung

body:
Wow that is an odd function. Please add a doc comment explaining it.

The name also doesn't really reflect what it does -- it seems to specifically assume that the pair has a pointer as its first component and a length as its second component? It would probably be less complicated to start by calling `ref_to_mplace`, and then converting the *place* to an allocation-range pair. That also makes a lot more sense in general: every `MPlace` does correspond to a range in some allocation. So I would suggest a signature like
```rust
fn mplace_to_alloc_range(..., &MPlaceTy<'tcx>)
-> (&Allocation, AllocRange)
```

This function has the extra twist that the pointer *must* point to already interned memory. It might not even need `&self` and can work with just a `tcx`?
author: RalfJung

body: The reason why I created this function was to get access to the `AllocRange` for the Allocation. Afaict there isn't a function that gets the `AllocRange` from an `MPlace`, so I had to get the length of the slice through the `ScalarPair`. We actually start out with an `MPlace`, so if I knew how to 'convert the place to an allocation-range pair' that would make this function unnecessary and would greatly simplify the logic.

What about types like `&(i32, [i32])`?

I think you might need something general for the *metadata* of a wide reference, but

author: RalfJung

body:
Thinking about it some more -- why do you even need to encode the length? It seems redundant. For `&[1,2,3]` at type `&[i32]`, I expect the valtree would be something like
```
   (root)
  /  |  \
1    2   3
```
This contains all the information needed to reconstruct the original value.
author: RalfJung

body: @RalfJung You're right, we can just read off the length of the slice from the length of the branch slice. Thanks for noticing that we don't actually need to encode the length in the valtree. But I still believe that we need to distinguish between refs corresponding to slices and other refs, because of the indexing into the place (we need to use `mplace_index` for slices and `mplace_field` for everything else), though we can do that in `branches` if you prefer that.

body:
```suggestion
    fn projection_opaque_must_outlive(
        &mut self,
        origin: infer::SubregionOrigin<'tcx>,
        region: ty::Region<'tcx>,
        generic: GenericKind<'tcx>,
    ) {
```
`projection_opaque_must_outlive` seems more clear naming as it is intended to handle the `GenericKind::{Opaque, Projection}` variants. `def_id`, `substs`, and `filter` args can be removed because they can easily be get from `generic`.

We can then inline `projection_must_outlive` and `opaque_must_outlive` in `type_must_outlive`.

It would also be nice if comments are updated to reflect how they're relevant to opaque types.

body:
Oh! At first I thought that there was a bug here, but I now see that we had this suggestion message as if it were inline, which it isn't (anymore?), making the wording out of order. We can reword these to be:

```suggestion
error[E0310]: the parameter type `A` may not live long enough
  --> $DIR/implied_lifetime_wf_check3.rs:24:41
   |
LL |     fn test<A>() where Ty<A>: 'static { assert_static::<A>() }
   |                                         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ type `A` doesn't meet its required lifetime bounds
   |
note: the lifetime is required by this bound
  --> $DIR/implied_lifetime_wf_check3.rs:23:25
   |
LL |     fn assert_static<A: 'static>() {}
   |                         ^^^^^^^
help: consider adding an explicit lifetime bound to satisfy the requirement
   |
LL |     fn test<A: 'static>() where Ty<A>: 'static { assert_static::<A>() }
   |              +++++++++
```
author: estebank

author: estebank

body:
Minimized to

```rust
fn foo<F>(_: F)
where
    F: 'static,
{
}

fn from<F: Send>(f: F) -> impl Send {
    f
}

fn bar<T>() {
    foo(from(|| ()))
}
```

giving the following error

```
error[E0310]: the opaque type `from<[closure@/home/ubuntu/rust4/src/test/ui/type-alias-impl-trait/implied_bounds3.rs:16:14: 16:19]>::{opaque#0}` may not live long enough
  --> /home/ubuntu/rust4/src/test/ui/type-alias-impl-trait/implied_bounds3.rs:16:5
   |
LL |     foo(from(|| ()))
   |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
   |
   = help: consider adding an explicit lifetime bound `from<[closure@/home/ubuntu/rust4/src/test/ui/type-alias-impl-trait/implied_bounds3.rs:16:14: 16:19]>::{opaque#0}: 'static`...
   = note: ...so that the type `impl Send` will meet its required lifetime bounds
```

This is because with this PR we don't consider the fact that calling `from` with `F` being the type of the `|| ()` closure, we should be able to know that the return type of `from` must outlive `'static` because `F` outlives `'static`. But we explicitly avoid inferring this kind of information, as it is unsound to do.

Now... the above snippet works on stable, so we shouldn't break it, especially since it *is* sound for return position impl trait. It's just that the very same thing would be unsound for return position impl trait.

Changing the example to

```rust
fn from<'a, F: Send + 'a>(f: F) -> impl Send + 'a {
    f
}
```

makes it compile again right now, since now we can sanely infer that the return type outlives `'static` just by knowing `F: 'static`.

Now... I'm going to add some logic to allow return position impl trait to look at its generic parameters to imply lifetime bounds that hold, just so we have back compat. But we need to look into it more to figure out whether this divergence between RPIT and TAIT is desirable.

body:
I didn't do it that way because so many of our current diagnostics don't have error codes, and adding an error code will increase the friction for converting to the derive (having to reserve an error code number makes a pull request more likely to have conflict and then you need to write the description for the error). 

Any error that currently has a code should be ported to a derive that has that code, but I don't personally mind if the derive doesn't force codes to be added where they didn't exist before, we can still make a mapping from error codes to slugs even if not all slugs had an error code.

Longer-term, it would be nice if we could have the error code index be generated from mandatory documentation comments on derive structs.

I don't feel too strongly here though, I can drop that commit (making error codes optional) if others from @rust-lang/wg-diagnostics prefer.

body:
I think you need to add an additional check that the trait that we're trying to suggest here is satisfied for all of the ADT's fields. 

You can do this by grabbing the ADT's ParamEnv using [`tcx.param_env`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/nightly-rustc/rustc_middle/ty/struct.TyCtxt.html#method.param_env), setting up a new obligation for each one of the trait's fields, and calling [`InferCtxt::predicate_must_hold_modulo_regions`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/nightly-rustc/rustc_trait_selection/traits/query/evaluate_obligation/trait.InferCtxtExt.html#tymethod.predicate_must_hold_modulo_regions). If we find a field that doesn't hold, then you can just bail.

Feel free to ping me on zulip if you need help setting this up -- it should significantly limit the cases which we suggest adding this `derive` attribute to only the ones where it generally makes sense to do so.

body:
So I was wrong here -- you need to fix two things:
1. Actually don't use `tcx.param_env`... you need to get the param-env from the obligation (from the function that calls `suggest_derive`).

2. The substs are not exactly correct for `PartialEq` and `PartialOrd`. Imagine this struct:
```rust
struct Foo {
  bar: String,
}
```
If you have `Foo: PartialEq<Foo>`, then you will currently create a child obligation for the `foo` field that looks like `String: PartialEq<Foo>`, instead of `String: PartialEq<String>`. Instead of using `mk_trait_obligation_with_new_self_ty`, you need to create a new trait ref with a new self type and possibly extra subst (for PartialEq and PartialOrd).

body:
The production used here are:

```
  <type> ::= <qualified-type>
         ::= P <type>        # pointer
         ...
         ::= <substitution>  # See Compression below
```

The `<type> ::= <qualified-type>` should create a separate substitution.

For example `pub extern "C" fn f(a: *const i32, b: *const *const i32) {}` is currently mangled as `_ZTSFvPKu3i32PKS0_E` which demangles to `typeinfo name for void (i32 const*, i32 const*)`.

body: The item name is not enough to uniquely identify a type. Different crates can have the same name but a different crate disambiguator. In addition you are missing the generic type parameters. I think you should just call into the type mangling code of the v0 symbol mangling scheme. At least for non-`#[repr(C)]` and non-primitive types. That includes Char, Str, Never, Tuple (except unit tuple), Slice, Foreign, FnDef, Closure, Generator, Ref and Dynamic, all of which are non-representable outside of rust. And finally I think for `#[repr(transparent)]` structs you should encode just the only non-ZST field as we guarantee it to be ABI compatible with this field. Eg `#[repr(transparent)] struct MyBool(bool, ());` and `bool` are guaranteed to be ABI compatible.

body:
> The item name is not enough to uniquely identify a type. Different crates can have the same name but a different crate disambiguator.

Yes, this is part of a larger discussion of how to identify C char and integer type uses for cross-language LLVM CFI support, and the solution will also apply to C void when implemented.

> In addition you are missing the generic type parameters.

Aren't types monomorphized at this time? Do you have an example and how I should handle it?

> I think you should just call into the type mangling code of the v0 symbol mangling scheme. At least for non-`#[repr(C)]` and non-primitive types. That includes Char, Str, Never, Tuple (except unit tuple), Slice, Foreign, FnDef, Closure, Generator, Ref and Dynamic, all of which are non-representable outside of rust.

I don't think we should be mixing encodings for CFI or using v0's encoding for several reasons but the main ones being separation of concerns (i.e., we shouldn't defer type membership grouping to v0 and its encoding), and losing control of how types are grouped, which will be needed in the future for implementing other features (e.g., increasing or decreasing granularity by adding or removing information, etc.).

> And finally I think for `#[repr(transparent)]` structs you should encode just the only non-ZST field as we guarantee it to be ABI compatible with this field. Eg `#[repr(transparent)] struct MyBool(bool, ());` and `bool` are guaranteed to be ABI compatible.

Thanks for pointing it out! I'll fix it.

body:
> If all types are monomorphized at this time, there won't be any substitutions, right?

The AdtDef is identical for all generic instances of the struct/enum/union. It only contains the definition you write as user. Eg for the type `Foo<u8>` it may contain `struct Foo<T> { a: T }`. The subsitutions are what distinguishes `Foo<u8>` and `Foo<u16>`.

> Is the crate disambiguator you're suggesting the one described in v0's RFC?

Pretty much.

> Would just including the crate name along the item name help?

No, the crate name is not guaranteed to be unique. Only the combination of crate name and crate disambiguator.

> What would happen with types that are re-exported in different crates but are expected to be interchangeable?

The `DefId` is the same. A `DefId` represents the actual definition, not the path by which you write it. Re-exports are resolved by the name resolution code.
author: bjorn3

body: In addition to crate & crate disambiguator, generating unique identifiers for nominal types also requires fully qualified paths to the type (or equivalent information encoded through other means, e.g., a hash like in a legacy mangling).
author: tmiasko

body:
> > If all types are monomorphized at this time, there won't be any substitutions, right?
> 
> The AdtDef is identical for all generic instances of the struct/enum/union. It only contains the definition you write as user. Eg for the type `Foo<u8>` it may contain `struct Foo<T> { a: T }`. The subsitutions are what distinguishes `Foo<u8>` and `Foo<u16>`.

I see. Thanks for pointing it out!

> 
> > Is the crate disambiguator you're suggesting the one described in v0's RFC?
> 
> Pretty much.
> 
> > Would just including the crate name along the item name help?
> 
> No, the crate name is not guaranteed to be unique. Only the combination of crate name and crate disambiguator.
> 
> > What would happen with types that are re-exported in different crates but are expected to be interchangeable?
> 
> The `DefId` is the same. A `DefId` represents the actual definition, not the path by which you write it. Re-exports are resolved by the name resolution code.

The LowerTypeTests pass just aggregates pointers by unique identifiers. It doesn't demangle or parse these ids, so it's unaware of name or type resolution (or DefIds), which means the encoding for re-exported types have to be exactly the same as the original type; otherwise, function pointers of functions that have parameters of the same type but with different paths won't be aggregated in the same group.
author: rcvalle

body:
This isn't demangled or parsed at any time and also is the `<name>` part of `u<length><name>[I<element-type1..element-typeN>E]` as a vendor extended type, which would be fine if it were to be. Using a hash as `<name>` is as arbitrary as this[^1], wouldn't change/disambiguate/increase the uniqueness of the ids, but we'd lose control over granularity.

Additionally, if we were to use a hash, since it's a security feature, we'd need to have control over all or most aspects of it. I.e., what and how it's hashed, what hashing function is used, its collision resistance, and so on. I prefer to continue using this instead if it's okay for you.

What are the components that are missing possible substitutions so I can try to add it?

[^1]: Which is not exactly arbitrary; it implements v0's `<path>` for possible cases only, and to be used as the `<name>` part of `u<length><name>[I<element-type1..element-typeN>E]` as a vendor extended type.

The `GENERALIZE_POINTERS` option is intended to offer behaviour compatible with `-fsanitize-cfi-icall-generalize-pointers`, right?

In terms of implementation strategy, I think it should be a type level transformation, independent of an actual mangling process. Additionally, like in Clang it should apply only to the parameters with pointer types and pointer return type. In contrast to the current implementation which generalizes all pointer types, everywhere.
author: tmiasko

body: The Clang implementation preserves the qualifiers of pointed-to type. That is, `*mut T` should generalize to `*mut ()` and `*const T` should generalize to `*const ()` (presuming that the unit type would be used to represent void).

body:
I would like to see a concrete use cases that you are trying to address with this special handling for `#[repr(transparent)]`. Examples of Rust code that would be rejected otherwise, ideally included as test cases. 

The current implementation mixes different concerns, those related to type identity and type mangling. I think those aspect should be separated. For example, the current implementation on the one hand mangles transparent types in terms of the inner type, on the other it still uses `ty::Ty` as a key which does distinguish between them, so they still generate distinct type identifiers  (it does use `ty0` as key here, but consider for example a reference to this newtype, and and a reference to the inner type).

Which is why I would prefer to leave this aspect for another pull request.
author: tmiasko

Hmm, so if I understand correctly, if one would like to use `#[repr(C)]` type for FFI purposes, one would have to guarantee that the type has the same name on Rust side and C/C++ side (and in C++ additionally ensure that the type is not nested in a namespace)?

The `#[repr(C)]` types are often used for their well-known layout, rather than for FFI purposes (e.g., search for `#[repr(C)]` in a tokio repository). Such types can be have generic parameters, and with this approach we lose the ability to distinguish between different generic arguments.

This implementation has also cache key issue similar to the one mentioned earlier.
author: tmiasko

body:
This should be a separate transformation step, independent of mangling.

Right now in the example below, `f1` and `g1` have the same typeid, while for `f2` and `g2` typeids are distinct.

```rust
pub fn f1(_: *const ()) {}                // _ZTSFvPKvE
pub fn g1(_: *const core::ffi::c_void) {} // _ZTSFvPKvE

pub fn f2(_: *const (), _: *const ()) {}                // _ZTSFvPKvS0_E
pub fn g2(_: *const (), _: *const core::ffi::c_void) {} // _ZTSFvPKvPKvE
```

author: tmiasko

body:
> The `GENERALIZE_POINTERS` option is intended to offer behaviour compatible with `-fsanitize-cfi-icall-generalize-pointers`, right?
Yes.

> 
> In terms of implementation strategy, I think it should be a type level transformation, independent of an actual mangling process.

While generalize pointers was made specifically to solve a problem with Clang's use of Itanium C++ ABI mangling for CFI (see https://clang.llvm.org/docs/ControlFlowIntegrity.html#fsanitize-cfi-icall-generalize-pointers), I see the benefits of doing a type transformation instead, but this would still belong to and depend on the encoding (e.g., other encodings may drop type and extended type qualifiers, generalize all pointer types, etc.).

> Additionally, like in Clang it should apply only to the parameters with pointer types and pointer return type. In contrast to the current implementation which generalizes all pointer types, everywhere.

In the current implementation, the `GENERALIZE_POINTERS` option (which is not enabled by default and doesn't have a driver and frontend option yet) will be set for `typeid_for_fnabi` and `typeid_for_fnsig` only, when the driver and frontend option is added and the feature is enabled.

body:
> Hmm, so if I understand correctly, if one would like to use `#[repr(C)]` type for FFI purposes, one would have to guarantee that the type has the same name on Rust side and C/C++ side (and in C++ additionally ensure that the type is not nested in a namespace)?

Yes, they'll have to use the same name for the type. Also, the Rust compiler provides interoperability with foreign code written in C, not C++. The latter has to be achieved via C wrappers. So, AFAIK, namespaced types can't used at the FFI boundary.

> 
> The `#[repr(C)]` types are often used for their well-known layout, rather than for FFI purposes (e.g., search for `#[repr(C)]` in a tokio repository). Such types can be have generic parameters, and with this approach we lose the ability to distinguish between different generic arguments.

Yes, unfortunately this is a trade off we'll have to make for cross-language CFI support (which is the most important goal).

Thanks for pointing it out! I'll fix it.

body:
I would like to understand why you think we shouldn't use v0 for functions with Rust calling convention. It still seems like very good choice to me, since it designed for Rust and with limited amount of implementation work we could have a first class support in demanglers.

> I don't think we should be mixing encodings for CFI or using v0's encoding for several reasons but the main ones being separation of concerns (i.e., we shouldn't defer type membership grouping to v0 and its encoding), and losing control of how types are grouped, which will be needed in the future for implementing other features (e.g., increasing or decreasing granularity by adding or removing information, etc.).

For example when you mention type membership grouping, I expect the type grouping to be implemented as type level transformation that selects a unique representative type out of an equivalence class of types. It would precede the type mangling process and be completely independent of it. Similarly to control granularity of encoding, we would omit the information from function signature.

I also don't understand what it means to "defer type membership grouping to v0 and its encoding", since v0 mangling doesn't group types.
author: tmiasko

body:
> I would like to understand why you think we shouldn't use v0 for functions with Rust calling convention. It still seems like very good choice to me, since it designed for Rust and with limited amount of implementation work we could have a first class support in demanglers.

This is where I think some of the confusion is coming from.These ids are never demangled and aren't supposed to be--they're only used by the LowerTypeTests pass to aggregate function pointers by unique ids.

> For example when you mention type membership grouping, I expect the type grouping to be implemented as type level transformation that selects a unique representative type out of an equivalence class of types. It would precede the type mangling process and be completely independent of it. Similarly to control granularity of encoding, we would omit the information from function signature.

This is also where I think some of the confusion is coming from.  We're implementing pointer type membership, more specifically function pointer type membership, not type membership--these are different concepts. A function return value and parameters type information aren't the only information that may be used to specify a function pointer type membership. So, this can't be entirely controlled only by a previous type transformation. For instance, we may want to add information about what registers are clobbered and what are preserved by a function. This can't be done with having control over only a previous type transformation.

> Similarly to control granularity of encoding, we would omit the information from function signature.

And what about adding granularity? How are we supposed to add information besides type information? Using the example I gave before, how would we add information about what registers are clobbered and what are preserved by a function?

> 
> I also don't understand what it means to "defer type membership grouping to v0 and its encoding", since v0 mangling doesn't group types.

That is exactly my point. We need an encoding implementation that groups function pointer types (not types) by whatever information is required for best CFI efficiency (not just type information).
author: rcvalle

body:
> This is where I think some of the confusion is coming from.These ids are never demangled and aren't expected to be--they're only used by the LowerTypeTests pass to aggregate function pointers by unique ids.

Yes, I understand that. Nevertheless I found the ability to demangle the type identifiers to be invaluable when trying to understand why some of resulting  identifiers are compatible and others aren't.

> For instance, we may want to add information about what registers are clobbered and what are preserved by a function. This can't be done with having control over only a previous type transformation.

Interesting. Indeed this wouldn't fit into this scheme, but ...

> And what about adding granularity? How are we supposed to add information besides type information? Using the example I gave before, how would we add information about what registers are clobbered and what are preserved by a function?

How would it be possible to add more information that isn't completely redundant with respect to information that is already contained in Rust type system? Functions with the same type are supposed to be compatible after all?
author: tmiasko
tmiasko

body:
> How would it be possible to add more information that isn't completely redundant with respect to information that is already contained in Rust type system? Functions with the same type are supposed to be compatible after all?

I gave an example already, but here's another example: Given three functions with the same return and parameter types A, B, and C, and an indirect call at location L to a function with the same return and parameter types as A, B, and C. We may want in the future, if it's determined that it isn't possible for C to be a called at location L, to group C in a different group than A and B. (Although I think it could be done during type transformation by synthesizing types to force these functions into different groups, if possible, this is beyond the type transformation's concern, and may result in ambiguity if not done properly.)

The threat model and threat(s) that control flow protection mitigates are at a lower level than type information, and we shouldn't constrain ourselves to it. Ideally, we should control all aspects of the mitigation, from code generation, type transformation, encoding, to even optimization passes (e.g., for instance, to avoid data loaded into registers that was supposed to be immutable from spilling onto the stack, such as the group ids, and later reused for type checks) to avoid introducing new CFI bypasses into the Rust compiler implementation, and to create opportunities to iterate on and improve it later.
author: rcvalle

body:
The motivation for my question was to understand the design choice of Itanium type mangling, instead of v0 mangling for functions with Rust calling convention (where the rationale for compatibility with C/C++ doesn't exists) - so it was essentially question about mangling of type information. If the information is of other nature, I don't see how it motivates the use of one mangling scheme over the other?

Anyway, as far as Rust calling convention is concerned, it doesn't need to be compatible with Clang and we can change it at any point. The current state seems fine, even though I still feel that v0 mangling would be preferable.
author: tmiasko

body:
It's not about whether to use v0's encoding or not, it's about using current v0's implementation, and more importantly, implementing or using multiple encodings (i.e., mixing encodings). Let me try to summarize:

Why not use current v0's implementation?

* Separation of concerns. v0's currently implementation's concern is mangling symbols for linking, where this encoding's concern is encoding type ids for grouping function pointers for best CFI efficiency. From v0's RFC:
    
      The scheme must provide an unambiguous string encoding for everything that can end up in a binary's symbol table.

  Encoding type ids for grouping function pointers require ambiguity in some cases, and introducing changes to current v0's implementation for that goes against its concern/requirement. (Although I think most of it could be done during type transformation, there are the other points about mixing encodings.) Conversely, changes to v0's implementation unrelated to CFI may go against best CFI efficiency.

Why not mix encodings?

* Reducing complexity and security risks. For security features, the less complexity the better. Implementing or using multiple encodings (and also changes to v0's implementation unrelated to CFI if using it) may result in unexpected behavior or introducing new CFI bypasses into the Rust compiler implementation.
* Reducing maintenance burden. We'd have to not only maintain feature parity between multiple encodings, but also guarantee that the resulting groupings of function pointers have equivalent properties between these encodings. (Either via type transformation or changing the encodings' implementation for non type information.)
author: rcvalle

body:
> It's not about whether to use v0's encoding or not, it's about using current v0's implementation ...

Thanks for explaining this. I didn't realize that you were referring to the existing implementation of v0 mangling.

> Encoding type ids for grouping function pointers require ambiguity in some cases, and introducing changes to current v0's implementation for that goes against its concern/requirement. (Although I think most of it could be done during type transformation, there are the other points about mixing encodings.)

A short comment about ambiguity and my earlier suggestion to separate type mapping / grouping from type mangling. In the current implementation those two concerns are tightly coupled. During mangling of a type, it might be transformed before mangling continues. The transformation are ambiguous in the sense that during mangling we will encounter distinct types that will eventually mangle the same way. In general that would be fine, but in the presence of a back references it becomes a correctness concern (we need to ensure that the same transformation is applied consistently to types stored / looked up in a type dictionary). Types that mangle the same way have to be treated as fully equivalent at this stage, any ambiguity at this point is a bug. The suggestion to separate those two concerns arisen primarily as a solution to this problem.
author: tmiasko

body:
> A short comment about ambiguity and my earlier suggestion to separate type mapping / grouping from type mangling. In the current implementation those two concerns are tightly coupled. During mangling of a type, it might be transformed before mangling continues. The transformation are ambiguous in the sense that during mangling we will encounter distinct types that will eventually mangle the same way. In general that would be fine, but in the presence of a back references it becomes a correctness concern (we need to ensure that the same transformation is applied consistently to types stored / looked up in a type dictionary). Types that mangle the same way have to be treated as fully equivalent at this stage, any ambiguity at this point is a bug. The suggestion to separate those two concerns arisen primarily as a solution to this problem.

Yes, I thought about two solutions for this substitution dictionary key issue: using a type's encoding string as the key for the dictionary, or doing the type transformation. Both would require traversing a type tree once (either for prepopulating the substitution dictionary, or for transforming types) before traversing it again for encoding. Doing the type transformation is a more elegant solution, and seems the right thing to do. I'll implement it soon.

Hey @lcnr , if and when you get around to removing this, what will be the recommended way to query for multi-component attributes?

We have a compiler tool with a late lint pass that checks for the presence of some `foo::bar` attribute on items, and it seems like if you get rid of this function, I won't be able to query for that since `PartialEq<Symbol> for Path` wants the path to have exactly one component.

I don't think so. If I `get_attrs` with `attr = Symbol("foo")`, then that calls `a.has_name("foo")` for each attribute, which does `a.path == "foo"`, and that `==` wants `path` to have exactly one component:

https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/ebf95836e3cfc8c442cc71239324d947aafa3eac/compiler/rustc_ast/src/ast.rs#L103-L108

body:
There is something *very* subtle going on here: This modifies the `Definitions` table which contains the sources for the `source_span` and `expn_that_defined` queries. So we are basically writing to the query tables of these queries here -- not literally but it amounts to the same thing. 

That should not be a problem because those queries are `eval_always`. But I think it would not even a problem otherwise: it looks like this is essentially the case that Niko described for Salsa 2, i.e. it is OK for a query A to write directly to the tables of other queries if the query key for those other entries contains something that A has created.

I think we should document this somewhere. Either here or on the queries in question.

body: @richkadel this pessimizes the hash used in coverage.  I could not find any documentation for this.  It is a big deal if the hash changes more often than strictly required?
author: cjgillot

body:
It's not well documented. [This document](https://llvm.org/docs/CoverageMappingFormat.html) says it's a "structural hash". I believe you want to make sure the hash does not change if the function source code does not change, and that the hash does change if the source changes.

What defines a change? Well, that's not clear. Since they call it a "structural hash", it _sounds_ to me like whitespace changes shouldn't count. But whitespace changes would affect lines and columns in coverage, so I'm not sure.

You said the hash may change "more often than strictly required". If by "more often" you mean it may sometimes change when it shouldn't, but usually it will be the same if the source doesn't change, then it's probably not a big deal.
author: richkadel

body:
> You said the hash may change "more often than strictly required". If by "more often" you mean it may sometimes change when it shouldn't, but usually it will be the same if the source doesn't change, then it's probably not a big deal.

Thanks. I confirm that with this implementation, the hash will take into account spans and white space. Furthermore, the hash for a closure can change because of an unrelated modification in the same function. It hashes strictly more information.

body:
The main point of these functions is to support legacy code that can't work with `addr` and `with_addr`. I don't think we can just impose constraints like this on such code.

Note that this is very fragile, e.g. consider a crate defining a newtype like `NonNull` that wraps raw pointers and all their operations -- then all 'expose'ing would seem to come from that same crate, even though it's actually acting "on behalf of" some other crate.

author: the8472

body:
I don't really know what `get_unchecked_mut` has to do with it :sweat_smile:  The safety comments explicitly clarify that "out of bounds" is referrign to the slice length as it gives the example of: `0 <= mid <= self.len()` having to hold. The problem is that for the `&mut [T]` that invariant implies that you can offset the ptr and it be inbounds the allocation, but for `*mut [T]` that does not hold.

The `SAFETY` on `&mut [T]::split_at_mut_unchecked` imo could be considered somewhat incomplete reasoning wise. it just states `` // SAFETY: Caller has to check that `0 <= mid <= self.len()`.`` but the complete reasoning here includes the fact that `&mut [T]` means that the ptr points to an allocation of size `self.len() * size_of::<T>()` therefore offseting the ptr by `mid` must be inbounds.

with `*mut [T]::split_at_mut_unchecked` that "unspoken" logic is broken and so either
- we must not rely on it inside `*mut [T]::split_at_mut_unchecked` i.e. use `.wrapping_add`
OR
- we must add the invariant to the safety comments i.e. "the pointer must point to an allocation of atleast size `mid * size_of::<T>()`. Then if we look at `*mut [T]::split_at_mut` we can see that the invariant is not fulfilled (and then we can fix that by removing the method or making it unsafe)

I think this is the right thing to do anyway, because #95488 isn't a tracking issue.

On the other hand, the only concrete advantage of having separate feature gates is to allow both features to be stabilized separately, but I don't think it would make sense here. body:
I just realized that the relocation may actually be superficial -- at least for ELF targets:
* The ELF spec reads:
  > When the link editor searches archive libraries, it extracts archive members that contain 
definitions of undefined global symbols.
  
  there is no mention that the symbol needs to be used -- from linker implementation POV I can see why this is specified like this.
* I just verified that both GNU ld and LLD behaves the same when I remove the relocation part and just keep the symbol table.

I didn't verify the behaviour of Mach-O and COFF.
author: nbdd0121

body: I mean the relocation itself is not valid for TLS symbols AFAIK.
author: bjorn3

body:
No. Because rlibs are archives, each object file in an archive needs to satisfy at least one undefined reference to be selected for linking. This [example(https://gist.github.com/nbdd0121/0f0bae5f4e3e2b3b8695f51eee523f19) doesn't print "B" even though it's declared a ctor.

`.init_arrays` is not *that* special -- it's also kept alive by `KEEP` directive in the default linker script.

body: Yeah, so removing the relocation at all would avoid the issue.

body:
The `.init_arrays` definition would get `#[used]`, right? So for

```
extern "C" fn my_constructor() {}

#[used]
#[link_section = ".init_array"]
static INIT_MY_CONSTRUCTOR: [extern "C" fn(); 1] = [my_constructor];
```

`my_constructor` wouldn't need to be explicitly included, but `INIT_MY_CONSTRUCTOR` would thanks to `#[used]`.

body:
pondering: since `read` doesn't have to return everything at once, maybe take advantage of that?

This could be something like
```rust
    let source = self.as_slices().0;
    let n = source.len().min(buf.len());
    buf[..n].copy_from_slice(&source[..n]);
    self.drain(..n); // I wish there was an `rtruncate`
    Ok(n)
```

That way the `read` is always just a single `memcpy`.  And `read_exact` will get the rest of the data in the second `read` call, if needed, since `as_slices` always returns the empty slice as `.1`, if it happens to be contiguous.

(I'm not confident that LLVM's optimizer can split the zip-drain into two `memcpy`s.  And sure, you could write the one-or-two `memcpy`s yourself, but since the caller can't rely on it anyway, might as well make it `read_exact`'s problem.  Arguably it even makes it a *better* mock to sometimes do incomplete `read`s...)
author: scottmcm

body:
I would like to do that, but how would I do it in a one-liner? The truth is basically this:

> `unsafe` functions do not implement the `Fn` family of traits, because you need to discharge the proof obligation on the unsafe functions by using an `unsafe {}` block in order to call it safely, and since proof obligations are not a part of the type system, a generic function invoking a trait cannot possibly know what the proof obligation is

Yeah, I'm not putting that in an error message.
author: notriddle

body:
Well, "`unsafe` functions do not implement `{Trait}` because they cannot be called generically as safe functions without an `unsafe` block" or something shorter might do. Then maybe change the note to "to make this into something implementing `{Trait}`, wrap it in a closure with an unsafe block".

I don't particularly like giving the advice to just wrap the call in an `unsafe` block without explaining _why_, since it's not advice I think people should just follow blindly.

body:
This is causing a failure when compiling `rustc_llvm`, and I'm not sure how to best handle it. The build script of that crate sets the `llvm_component` for every LLVM component enabled as part of the build:

https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/634770c0a7f8598164ab825cfe419cc8b03c36e5/compiler/rustc_llvm/build.rs#L175-L177

The list of available components is also quite long:

https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/634770c0a7f8598164ab825cfe419cc8b03c36e5/compiler/rustc_llvm/build.rs#L134-L163

My understanding is that, since some of those flags won't be passed, we need to add them to the list of allowed flags:

https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/634770c0a7f8598164ab825cfe419cc8b03c36e5/src/bootstrap/lib.rs#L191-L225

I'm worried that the list is quite long though, and it might be annoying keeping it up to date. Is there a better way?

cc @Urgau
author: emilyalbini

body: If it works, then sure yes. If you don't want to do it I could do it in a follow-up PR.
author: Urgau

body:
Oh, sorry for that.

> I'm worried that the list is quite long though, and it might be annoying keeping it up to date. Is there a better way?

I think it could be done directly in the `build.rs` instead of adding it to the global extra. Something like this might work:

```rust
println!("cargo:rustc-flags=-Zunstable-options");
println!("cargo:rustc-flags=--check-cfg=values(llvm_component, ...)");
```

Ideally there would be a dedicated cargo build script command for that but it's not yet implemented. 

EDIT: Cargo doesn't allow it, looking for another solution.

author: lqd

body:
I think it's because the name is [computed once in preparation](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/ed6c958ee4bf081deec951996ace9c508360c1d9/compiler/rustc_codegen_llvm/src/back/lto.rs#L78), and then used in different places in fat and thin LTO: 
- concatenated with other modules for different purposes (cached modules, serialized modules, green modules, etc)
- or as e.g. the module identifier for LLVM functions and data structures (esp. in ThinLTO), or in error handling (where `CString` also feels out-of-place) and debug logging like here. 

I suppose this was done to avoid many different conversions (and/or done at a time before `rustc_codegen_ssa` had a `ModuleCodegen` with a String name, and so on), but looking around there's quite a few conversions present still: a bit of cleanup could be interesting in this area, but a bunch of these conversions are between the `cg_ssa` String names and FFI, so there may be only little we can do. It seems easy to turn fat LTO to use Strings, but less so for ThinLTO).

I may look at that in another PR if that's OK ?

body:
The same reason why you would shout "Watch out for that car!" instead of saying it gently; because it is more likely to be noticed. I feel that an assertion failure is more serious than just an error message, because it is unexpected and so you may not be paying much attention.

But I am sure there are conventions for this sort of thing already and either way would make me happy. The important part is that there should be some message instead of no message.

author: Amanieu

body: What happens if the reader count overflows?
author: Amanieu

body: The `readers()` function looks like it extracts the readers count, meaning the value doesn't consider existing waiting threads. Having a writer locked means the readers count would be the maximum value so the `.then()` clause would short-circuit early and return `None`. The use of `+` over `|` is a bit misleading, but that just transitions from 0 readers to max readers (a.k.a writer).
author: kprotty

body: `read_lockable()` checks for `MAX_READERS` which looks like its writer value minus one. If the reader count would overflow into a writer, it looks like that would be detected by `read_lockable()` and short circuit to `None`.

Hmm, so this fixes your immediate issue, but it surprises me that it works - it's going to just unconditionally use `--cfg bootstrap` now, even though it's still using the host clippy. Maybe it didn't come up because this is so soon after the beta bump?

I think ideally the fix would look something like:
- Download clippy for the bootstrap toolchain in `bootstrap.py` (look for `add_component`)
- Remove all this hacky code running rustc --version; treat it just the same as --cfg=bootstrap for `build` and `check`.
- Either put a `todo!()` for stages other than 0, or build `tool::Clippy` in-tree before running them, rather than the host clippy.

body: I think this is due to the way typeck handles errors.  `rustc_typeck::check_crate` aborts compilation earlier in case a diagnostic has been emitted.  As `resolve_lifetimes` was called by typechecking, the diagnostics emitted by lifetime resolution were triggered a compilation abort. Now that diagnostics are emitted elsewhere, compilation continues normally.

body:
This is reasonable while we are still matching the macro definition against `$( $lhs:tt => $rhs:tt );+` reusing `macro_rules` machinery.

However, we really should stop pretending that `macro_rules! foo { ... }` or `macro foo (...) { ... }` are macro invocations.
This involves removing all the comments saying that, introducing a custom parser for the `$( $lhs:tt => $rhs:tt );+` syntax (and its `macro` 2.0 equivalent), and storing the output from such parser (the lowered macro representation) to crate metadata, so that macro definitions are not reparsed as raw token streams by every dependent crate.

A comment explaining the return value here would be useful:

body: It seems a bit strange to represent `WRITE_LOCKED` with a special reader count. I think using a separate bit for `WRITE_LOCKED` would make the code more efficient (e.g. using native overflow flags to detect reader count overflow, using `fetch_or` for locking instead of a CAS loop, etc).

body:
Putting them in the high bits makes it possible for the read condition to compile down to a single comparison:

`readers(state) < MAX_READERS && !readers_waiting(state) && !writers_waiting(state)`

is then equal to

`(state as u32) < 0x3ffffffe`.

(Not sure if llvm sees that though.)

body: It's to outline the slow path so that the fast path can be inlined. This is a codegen technique used in [fast](https://github.com/golang/go/blob/master/src/sync/mutex.go#L209-L215) [synchronization](https://github.com/Amanieu/parking_lot/blob/master/core/src/word_lock.rs#L103-L107) [primitives](https://github.com/kprotty/usync/blob/master/src/rwlock.rs#L162-L175).
author: kprotty

body:
Checking the state of the lock need not be ordered before or after other memory operations here. The `compare_exchange` has Acquire only to ensure that:
- memory operations before previous unlocks which do a Release happen before the lock is observed to be held
- the lock being held happens before the memory operations in its critical section.
author: kprotty

author: m-ou-se

body: Sure. I don't think it really matters though. Have you ever seen llvm generate anything more efficient when switching from compare_exchange to compare_exchange_weak in a loop? It doesn't actually turn a loop with compare_exchange_weak into a basic ll/sc loop, and still uses a regular load at the start of the loop and only uses the load-link (ldaxr) at the end of the loop with a separate compare and branch-not-equal before the store-conditional. (Because otherwise it can't guarantee that the linked load and store are close enough to each other to guarantee progress.)
author: m-ou-se

body: weak allows it to run loop conditions more frequently (on stxr failure instead of on cmp failure/clrex). But that shouldn't make a difference here as the condition would be the same in the strong case.
author: kprotty

body: On platforms using LL/SC you will end up with 2 nested loops: the outer loop + an inner loop for the strong compare_exchange. You can eliminate the inner loop by using compare_exchange_weak. This isn't too significant here since this is the slow path, but it is significant in the inline fast path.
author: Amanieu

body: Every FUTEX_WAKE operation (on Linux, *BSD, wasm, etc.) returns the number of awoken threads. This just only wakes one thread at most, so we use a boolean instead of a i32 or usize. I've added doc comments.
author: m-ou-se

body:
I tried it, but it does not seem to simplify things.

`fetch_or` doesn't seem very useful, because it should only set the bit if the lock isn't read-locked yet. If we unconditionally set the bit when attempting to lock, it will also be set when it's still read-locked and read-unlocking will need special handling for that situation.

Simplifying the overflow check doesn't help, because at the points where this is checked, we also need to check the waiting bits too.

I also prefer to combine the fields, to make invalid states unrepresentable. (Effectively `enum { Unlocked, ReadLocked(NonZeroNonMaxU30), WriteLocked }` instead of `struct { read_locked: u29, write_locked: bool }`.)
author: m-ou-se

body:
LLVM doesn't currently see this, although it sees a related pattern where the `0x3ffffffe` mask has only one set bit.

Reported upstream: https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/54856

(Note that I looked at `readers(state) < MAX_READERS & !readers_waiting(state) & !writers_waiting(state)` with bitwise and--LLVM has more trouble with logical and.)

This doesn't seem any easier-to-follow than the previous version. Here's the things that I really don't like about either version:

1. It uses `indent.saturating_sub(1)`. Does this mean `left_padding` has the same value when `indent=0` as it has when `indent=1`? If so, isn't that a bug? If not, why not?
2. This is doing string replacement on a value that was already produced with string concatenation above. Isn't this slow? Why isn't it building the string correctly the first time? I would imagine setting up `br_with_padding` at the beginning of the function and then using it below, instead of doing this `clause.replace()` stuff, would be faster (though, perhaps, not faster-enough to show up in profiling).
3. `clause = left_padding + &clause` and `clause.insert_str(0, left_padding)` are the same thing. One isn't cleaner than the other.

All direct calls to `comma_sep` are in this file, but tracking down the inputs that are eventually fed into `comma_sep` seems troublesome.

Rather than shoving these into a catch-all statement, you should just annotate the match arms with these attributes:

```rust
#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]
libc::SIGSTKFLT => "SIGSTKFLT",
```

This should work as-is but you might have to enable a feature flag since I'm not sure if it's stable.

However, doing this will also make sure that the match gets compiled to a single lookup table instead of performing sequential checks.

+ by placing the `T` returned from your closure into the allocation.
```

In general I think we should avoid such 'f.a.q. style' questions in API documentation. If there's obvious questions left after the explanation, then we should find a way to include the missing information there, such that readers are not left with questions that need to be addressed separately.

body:
Please document all the considerations here. Basically we want a quick reject/accept in all the cases, so

* `eq`
    * can return `false` if the hashes differ
    * can return `true` if the pointers are the same
    * only if neither of these did anything do we need to check the actual data behind the pointer
* `ne`
    * can return `true` if the hashes differ
    * can return `false` if the pointers are the same
    * only if neither of these did anything do we need to check the actual data behind the pointer
author: oli-obk

body:
In my eyes nondeterminism is kind of a poor argument here, considering that in general this function *is* nondeterministic at runtime in the sense that your allocation/stack whatever could be aligned to different things from one execution to the next, right? I think many folks would probably also respond to this with "ok, sure, but I'm fine with it".

It also seems to me that if we really wanted to, we probably could do "better" (e.g., if we guaranteed that all linker-level allocations were 8-byte-aligned, and then propagated that information down, creating something like monomorphized constants by the "parent" alignment). It's just actually doing that is very hard and not particularly worthwhile (you'd end up with a lot of duplicate work I'd guess).

Is there a reason we need this paragraph, instead of moving directly to the next one, which makes no real argument for *why* this is the case, aside from justifying it being OK to not worry about it since "we're not doing const SIMD anyway"?
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
Sure, we can constrain the non-determinism, and then some more things become possible. But others remain impossible (e.g. in your case, asking about any alignment >8).

The problem is not that allocation non-determinism makes the behavior of this function non-deterministic. The problem is that, as a `const fn`, this function is executed *before allocation non-determinism has been resolved*. Like, we'd literally have to predict the future to be able to always implement this as a `const fn`.
author: RalfJung

body: Yeah, that makes sense. I think we could probably tackle all realistic programs in a similar way, but it's a pile of worms and a bunch of complexity. In any case, I'm not strictly opposed to the wording here, but it also feels like it's an unnecessary diversion -- what do you think about just skipping this middle paragraph, and moving directly to the lack of need for it, i.e., const not supporting SIMD etc anyway?

body: I personally would leave a short note saying that const-eval runs before addresses are picked, so the final alignment if unknoweable, and that's why this operation always "fails" during `const`.

> Is there a reason we need this paragraph, instead of moving directly to the next one, which makes no real argument for why this is the case, aside from justifying it being OK to not worry about it since "we're not doing const SIMD anyway"?

*puppy-dog eyes* b-b-b-but
author: workingjubilee

body:
More directly: given a slice with a random position in memory, we can't know the head of the slice is SIMD-aligned, and given a slice, we can have any number of values. Thus we **must** have a head and tail handling routine, because asserting on the size is not good enough (may be off-alignment) and asserting on the alignment is not good enough (may be an uneven number of elements, thus not trivially vectorized without tail-handling), and asserting on both just gives you a panic generator where a useful program should be, because you usually don't really have any way to pre-establish those guarantees in a useful manner based on the type information currently present in the system. And if you actually do, you have much bigger tricks to deploy on your code than this.

Thus, "you better believe you are going to be writing code to handle this correctly, Miri's semantics be damned."
author: workingjubilee

body:
In my experience, SIMD programmers don't actually care about Miri's needs that much. It's not like they hate Miri, it just doesn't help them understand why they have to handle this. They want to instead hear something like,

"Yes, the promises are technically very weak, but this function will exercise a good faith effort to work as you would expect it to when your code actually runs on a **hard processor**. But if your code is executed on Miri's virtual machine, Miri is allowed to do really really weird things, like put all the data of your program in virtual registers instead of truly addressable virtual memory, and then give you pointers into the registers, and then alignment doesn't truly exist? It's... it's weird.

Just write code that is sound and this method will give it approximately the performance characteristics you would want at runtime. But if it is executed during CTFE, then the performance characteristics don't really matter, because the actual runtime cost becomes zero, and no matter how much SIMD acceleration you bring, you can't beat zero time."

body:
This isn't exactly right: A `&str` is allowed to contain invalid utf-8, but other functions might assume that a `&str` is valid utf-8, making a non-utf-8 `&str` very hard to safely use. But just calling this function with invalid utf-8 is, by itself, not unsafe.

See also e.g. https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/std/string/struct.String.html#method.from_utf8_unchecked
author: m-ou-se

body:
This is true, but this is perhaps even handled just by if the validity of `&T` is independent from whether the pointee bytes are valid at type `T`.

In general, `from_unchecked` functions do just say that it is UB to provide an argument which does not satisfy the safety invariant. This is useful, because it allows a sanitizing implementation of the function which checks the precondition.

A postcondition of "is not used in any way which causes UB" is *much* more difficult to reason about.

Another point is that `str` has the option of using `&*(v as *const [u8] as *const str)` to construct a `&str` to invalid-UTF-8. `String` doesn't have any such API, relying on conversion to/from `Vec<u8>`.

If any `str`/`String` methods actually documented that they were safe to call with a reference to invalid UTF-8, then this weaker documentation requirement makes sense. As is, the *only* possible thing to do with a `str`/`String` to invalid UTF-8 is to `forget` it. With that the case, the clearer precondition seems better to use.

body:
I think we should probably drop support for this being invoked from the CLI. It probably should in general be moved into the sequence of copies we always execute when building a sysroot (Step for Assemble), rather than being ad-hoc managed on demand here. That'll ensure we always have them in every sysroot. We'll probably still need kind of similar logic to the below to make sure they end up in dist tarballs, but I think this approach sounds cleaner to me overall.

(This seems like work for a separate PR, though; if you can file an issue, that'd be great).
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
It's intentional that "all" (modulo debugger scripts which are an outlier) of the dist steps correspond to a particular tarball we ship, rather than a path in src/ -- this was semi-recently changed and is I think the right approach. We're not really dist'ing src/tools/cargo *specifically* here.

The general expectation I have is that when you want to limit yourselves to a subset of things to dist, you probably do so with the end artifacts in mind rather than the inputs that may go into those (i.e., you go look at what is produced from x.py dist without additional args, and then pick a subset).

body:
This gets opened with `O_RDONLY` but unlinkat only needs write access to the directory so we should use some other permission flags here, e.g. `O_PATH` on linux. That's relevant in weird cases where one has `-wx` on the ancestor. Or even `-w-` if relative paths are involved and the ancestor sits above the current working directory. The UI test could test these edge-cases too.

Additionally we may want to allow failure here and just `stat('..')` instead to get the dev and ino of the parent without having to open it at all because there would be cases where the recursive version could remove an entire tree and only fail on the final unlink when the parent isn't accessible.

Another option is to keep the root fd open instead of its parent, that'll prevent its inode number from being recycled and we can dup the fd in that case instead of checking the grandparent.

body:
Its not clear to me how ascending to the grandparent is needed. Or perhaps its just a wording confusion problem.

https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/93160#discussion_r790290684 describes causing a privileged process to delete the contents of a sticky directory by moving.

1. `foo/bar/baz` is being deleted
2. Alice moves `bar` aside
3. Alice deletes `foo`
4. Alice waits for the inode `foo` had to be recycled into a desirable context, for instance as a new sticky dir `/somewhere/newtmp`
5. Alice moves `bar` into that dir
6. the priviled process moves up from `bar` to `/somewhere/newtmp`

At this point the *parent* of `/somewhere/newtmp` needs to be verified as being legitimate, so its (dev,inode) are cross-checked; the grandparent is not relevant.

But as I say perhaps I'm just misunderstanding :/. It seems to me that a rule of 'only operate (readdir/unlink_at its children) on a directory that was either a) opened going down or b) was reopened going up and still has its own original parent (inode,dev) seems quite robust: the greatest attack possible is to operate on newly created directories that have the same inode as they had before *and* are in the same containing directory-by-inode that they had before. That property holds because downward traversal to discover a directory inode must occur before inode reuse can be attempted.

On the other hand, if reuse attacks are something worth guarding against, I don't think that that property is sufficient: the privileged attacker *can* be fooled into deleting content that was not in the original file tree, and if that acts as a gadget into some other attack, we now have a chained attack possible.
author: rbtcollins

body:
> At this point the _parent_ of `/somewhere/newtmp` needs to be verified as being legitimate, so its (dev,inode) are cross-checked; the grandparent is not relevant.

That is what the code does. The comment is semantically located just _before_ going up, `current_readdir = parent_readdir` happens further down. So at the point of the comment all children of `current_dir` have been processed but it (still) points to `/somewhere/newtmp/bar`, `parent_dir` points to `/somewhere/newtmp` and `grandparent_readdir` will point to  `/somewhere`.

There is no "ascending to the grandparent". The invariant is that the current directory and its parent directory have the correct (dev, ino) and are kept open, while the children of the current directory are processed.

You are right that this does not guard against _all_ reuse attacks but it does make reuse attacks much harder. GNU rm does not protect against any of those attacks IIRC.

body:
I tried to do the `Cow` thing. It was a bit weird. First, I had to implement `ToOwned` for `mbe::TokenTree`. Second, it doesn't really match how `Cow` is supposed to work -- there is no possibility of a `Borrowed` value being converted to an `Owned` value, for example. Although I tried it, I didn't finish getting it to compile and run because partway through I realized that the custom `Clone` is a good idea, and good enough.

So I changed `TtHandle::Token` to contain a `mbe::TokenTree` instead of a `Token`, which got rid of the clumsiness for `TtHandle::get()`. And then I had to do a custom `Clone` for `TtHandle`, which was easy.

body:
This was part of the logic for printing the hint to run with `-h -v` to see available paths if and only if there are paths that we could list for that subcommand.

For example, in Rust now if you run `./x.py clippy -h` it will suggest `Run './x.py clippy -h -v' to see a list of available paths.`, but when you run `./x.py clippy -v -v` it doesn't actually list any paths.

My assumption was that all of these commands could have useful paths to pass to them but I might be mistaken about that. It seems like check and clippy would take paths just like build does, but it kinda seems like clippy runs for "everything" in the build graph anyway and passing paths doesn't do much.

Is this specific list of commands which could print a list of paths for help text intentional and the others (clippy, check, fix) are supposed to be excluded?

author: jyn514

body:
> My assumption was that all of these commands could have useful paths to pass to them but I might be mistaken about that.

This is not currently true. There are several related issues:
1. Not all commands accept paths. `x clippy`, as you noticed, runs on the whole tree. `x clean` does the same.
2. Some commands that do not accept paths *do not check whether a path was passed* - `x clippy src/bootstrap` is exactly the same as an unconditional `x clippy`.
3. Some commands the *do* accept paths do not go through `StepDescription`. For example `x fmt src/bootstrap` is perfectly valid and limits the changes to bootstrap itself, but there is no `impl Step for Format` description, because `Step` has no way to say "all paths".

Fixing those issues should wait for follow up PRs, I think. In the meantime, can you do the following:
- [ ] Remove `clippy` and `fix` from the `check` arm and down to the `vec![]` arm. `clippy` is certainly not supported with paths, and `fix` has been broken for ages.
- [ ] Change the code in `usage` that calls `get_help` to panic if `-h -v` is called for a subcommand that does not support paths. We should give the user an idea that something is broken rather than silently ignoring the flag.

body:
Is there not a third style here that combines the two, using the "expect as error message" style for the message and simply writing the precondition in a comment similar to a `// Safety: ` comment before an `unsafe` block?

I'm struggling to see the advantages of the "expect as precondition" style given that one would almost certainly look at the offending line when debugging an issue encountered by a user (or oneself) and thus come across the comment explaining the precondition. It removes one layer of indirection (the need to read through the code) but that seems like a fairly small win. I'd be much happier if expect messages would converge towards one style across the Rust ecosystem.
