body:
fix: I don't think we should mention specific external crates in the standard library docs, even if they're good ones by official working groups.  (Maybe there are exceptions for crates that come from `rust-lang` repos, but even then I'd expect it to be incredibly rare.)

If we want to link to something, I think it makes more sense to link to a particular [category](https://crates.io/category_slugs) instead -- that way it's a good link so long as that category is meaningful, rather than including a link to a deprecated crate for 6+ weeks if we get another `failure` situation where the suggested thing changes.  (There's not a category that mentions panic hooks right now, but we could fix that.  And maybe link a keyword for now.)
author: scottmcm

body: I like the strong language here, because it also fits well with a more general thing about saying that `.expect` is generally not supposed to be for user-visible stuff (though of course it *might* happen) since it's for things that you have a reason to expect won't happen, and that reason is what goes in the text.  (Said otherwise, result errors says what went wrong, expect messages are for saying why you think it shouldn't have happened.)

author: nagisa

body:
This implements a scheme known as round to nearest, ties away from zero. This is a pretty unusual rounding scheme at least as far as IEEE 754 floats are concerned. The usual rounding mode that people will expect is round to nearest, ties to even.

In order to correctly implement this, I believe you will need to inspect more less significant bits. In particular rounding up here is only correct if any of bits following the `$mant_bits - 1` is set. Otherwise the rounding direction needs to be decided based on the LSB of the mantissa.

And now that we're in the business of discussing how to handle ties, there's also a question of how many less-significant bits we should be looking at before deciding if a tie has occurred. I don't know what if IEEE 754 specifies this (and if it doesn't what implementations tend to do here.) I would imagine just considering the `$mant_bits..$mant_bits+2` would be sufficient, but to be honest I'm not quite sure.
author: nagisa
newpavlov

body:
I think it is critical that we implement the same tie resolution mechanism as used by the regular floating operations if we are implementing rounding-to-nearest for this specific operation at all.

Yes, implementing tie-away-from-zero is simpler. It is also true that the specific points raised in the referenced issue will get resolved no matter which tie resolution scheme is adopted here. I acknowledge that there are very few examples where the need to break ties comes up in the first place, and that the motivations behind picking a rounding mode for floating point operations doesn't apply here to the same degree.

What I'm worried about, however, is a situation in which somebody eventually comes up with some specific input that doesn't look “right”, would look right with a different tie resolution mode, and declares it a regression. With ties-away-from-zero their case would be that much stronger precisely because this scheme is so obscure.

Basically ties-to-even has a benefit of making it work exactly like the rest of the floating point operations, and in turn much easier to justify the specific choice for this function. If it doesn’t for some reason work right for somebody, it is then up to themselves to implement something that they think works right.

---

All that said, I'm be happy to leave the final call up to libs-api or some other T-libs team here.

body: Referring to something as `def_id` when it isn't actually a `DefId` seems bad. I know there are other spots in rustdoc where this is done; those are technical debt, and I'd like to see them go away over time, instead of perpetuating the confusion.
author: notriddle

body:
This function is a landmine, because its type signature is a lie. You can't just go from `ItemId -> DefId`, you need to know what you're looking at.

* If we know that your `Impl::def_id` function will always return something that actually has a `DefId`, then this function shouldn't be necessary. Make that other function actually return a `DefId`.
* If you don't know that, have this function return an `Option` instead, and everywhere it's called, add a comment justifying why, in that particular spot, you know that primitives will never show up.
* If you really can't come up with a good justification, then it's fine. `ItemId` implements `Hash`, so you can just make it an `FxHashMap<DefId, FxHashSet<ItemId>>` instead.
author: notriddle

body: Sorry, const prop is doing a lot of magic that I never really looked at. :/  In terms of just a MIR diff, what is this about?
author: RalfJung

body: This isn't really an MIR diff. Before this PR, const prop had a rule that it would not try to do any evaluation for things that were greater than some maximum size. If the return destination for function call terminators violated this rule, it would instead pass `None` (which was normally reserved for cases where the function is guaranteed to diverge), and presumably ensure that this doesn't cause problems elsewhere. Because passing `None` is no longer an option, I am instead passing an allocation of `()`, and hoping that whatever was preventing bugs before will continue to do so now.

We should officially not support intra-doc links in `<>`. It’s not documented, it’s buggy, and the `<>` symbols are already painfully overloaded. What's supposed to happen when someone tries to use a `` HTML tag, but they have a function named `b()` in scope? Just to get across how confusing it is, *you both got it wrong.* `` is not being parsed as an intra-doc link. It's an HTML tag. It's not getting stripped. It's right there in the generated HTML: ```html

This is a super https://crate.com .

```

body:
I left a comment about this above.
I collected some statistics from the standard library and the `[]` pair appeared exactly the same number of times as a single opening bracket `[` (about 4000 times, IIRC).
So if we are doing a conservative estimate we don't even need to check for the closing bracket, it's pretty much guaranteed to be there (which makes the check ~twice faster).

author: scottmcm

body:
You are referring to "possibly" -> "it will possibly eliminate"? I suggest keeping using strong language here because the programmer should always assume that the compiler *does* eliminate those branches, even if it technically doesn't, yet does other funky stuff which causes time travel and nose daemons.

Maybe more clear: "it will eliminate all branches in the surrounding code which the compiler can determine will invariably lead to a call to `unreachable_unchecked!`."

body:
> I have RUSTC_LOG=warn set in my zshrc I believe. This will cause be rather noisy I think.

Note that this is never printed by rustc, since `ERR_ON_PARTIAL_PTR_OVERWRITE` is true there.
Only Miri can ever trigger this.

> They alsodon't get optimized out, so nightly will have them

This is a very cold path, so that's fine. We have `info!` for each statement and terminator we run (also exists on nightly), this is at least as important as that.

When this triggers, it can cause subtly wrong execution later in the program, where memory is uninit that should be init -- so there should be a good way to figure out if this triggered.

> We kind of emit warn and error unconditionally, or at least used to at some point.

This is definitely not printed by default, I verified that.

body:
Technically, this is currently still redundant -- we could make this `ptr_get_offset` and have it return just a `Size`, and get all the other data directly from the `PointerTag`.

But in preparation for @carbotaniuman's PR, I think we should leave this the way it is. After that PR, `Provenance::get_alloc_id` should be called only for diagnostics and when `Provenance::OFFSET_IS_ADDR == false`, and `ptr_get_alloc` should be used throughout the machine to extract information from provenance. (And that's what we already do, so it would be silly to change it back and forth.)

body: was `debug_assert_ne`, but became `assert`, so will be called at not debug builds. Why this change?
author: klensy

body:
This makes it consistent with the unix version of `borrowed_fd`, which does a plain `assert`. Which is there because the previous unix implementation code `FileDesc::new` used a plain `assert`.

Also, I think it makes sense; this is a new API, and we're expecting to migrate mature codebases to use it, so it makes sense to be extra careful that old and new code agree on when and where `INVALID_SOCKET` can appear. Also, `OwnedSocket` uses `rustc_layout_scalar_valid_range_*` layout tricks that assume that `INVALID_SOCKET` can't appear, so it could easily produce some dramatic UB if the invariant is violated.

body:
In the mono repo, I found the value 1.

The LTOGenerator only adds the flag.
 https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/09c2b7c35af8c4bad39f03e9f60df8bd07323028/llvm/lib/LTO/LTOCodeGenerator.cpp#L541
author: tschuett

body:
The code here is based on the file tschuett linked above. In the LLVM repo that code is responsible to set up LTO. Adding this flag was missing from the Rust code that should emulate the code in `LTOCodeGenerator.cpp`.

The `11` argument in the `HasModuleFlag` is the size of the string passed to this function, not the value of the flag. The `1` argument in the `AddModuleFlag` function is the value of the module flag. LLVM doesn't need a string size here, because it expects a `\0` terminated string in this function.

body:
Thanks for pointing this out. I don't really have a solution for this right now. I tried looking at the reachability of the `trait_ref` and the members of the traits with the [`privacy_access_levels`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/nightly-rustc/rustc_middle/ty/context/struct.TyCtxt.html#method.privacy_access_levels) query. But for all of the traits and the functions in the traits (queried with `tcx.vtable_entries`) this just returns `None`, meaning it is not reachable IIUC.

I guess I would somehow have to check the reachability for the `dyn Trait` types. But I couldn't figure out how to do that.

Do you have ideas on how to best address this issue?

body:
The ICE is due to the `description_and_note` being called everytime something unsafe is happening, even when that unsafety is valid (e.g in valid unsafe block/function). Because we use `def_path_str` in a non-erroring result, and the underlying implementation is expensive, we trigger a sanity check for just that: `delayed_good_path_bugs` 

What I don't understand is how the diag emit in `check_unsafety` is not shown to the user all the time

Why is it done this way? Seems wasteful.
author: Emilgardis

body: You could move the call at https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/8d8135f003b35c3e109d013b2bed9ee9496da615/compiler/rustc_mir_build/src/check_unsafety.rs#L73 into the two match arms below that actually use it.

Ah, yeah [I thought](https://rust-lang.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/182449-t-compiler.2Fhelp/topic/ICE.20in.20stage.201.20build.20of.20pr.20.2396294/near/279869133) this was the reason too, but it's not, that's `UnsafeOpKind::description_and_note`, the only time we call `UnsafetyViolationDetails::description_and_note` is in [`rustc_mir_transform::check_unsafety::check_unsafety`](https://github.com/Emilgardis/rust/blob/9893ee4c363cff47bc3b1dcd4af099ac16fc3fdf/compiler/rustc_interface/src/passes.rs#L961), and that function is only called [here](https://github.com/Emilgardis/rust/blob/9893ee4c363cff47bc3b1dcd4af099ac16fc3fdf/compiler/rustc_interface/src/passes.rs#L961) in `MIR_effect_checking`

body:
This feels off to me -- previously the logic had the equivalent of nth(self.n - 1), nth(n) here, I think? But now we're doing nth(self.n) and then nth(n), which is off by one.

Do we have tests for this function? I'm a little surprised this didn't trigger any change in them.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
I'm not sure exactly how this should be tested. This PR primarily intends to reduce the amount of unnecessary intermediate loading of values, but this one can't necessarily be avoided because some value has to be loaded in-between `0..usize::MAX` on overflow to make sure the iterator is fully consumed but does not return the value at `usize::MAX`.

The index was off by one, but a test would have to be written to check that the intermediate value discarded was at the "right" place, is this valuable? I'll just open the PR and someone can comment if tests should be added in a specific way.
author: austinabell

body: Please also avoid merge commits and instead [rebase](https://rustc-dev-guide.rust-lang.org/git.html#rebasing-and-conflicts).
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body: There's a slight behavior change in this code if the inner iterator panics; previously we'd have already set self.n to zero, but now we don't. It's probably fairly easy to preserve the old behavior, but also likely not that important, so seems OK.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body: If this returns `None`, we used to early-exit, but now the code will call self.iter.nth(n) and potentially return something on a non-Fused iterator. I think we should preserve that behavior and add a test for this condition.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
My impression is that the index being off by one means that an iterator would return a *different* element. For example, an iterator over (0..=u128::MAX).skip(usize::MAX).nth(usize::MAX) should return u128::MAX, but I think with the previous code would have returned u128::MAX-1.

Can you add a test for this?
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body: no, that's fair, fixed

body: good catch, my mistake for not being thorough! Test added

The check you mentioned shouldn't return `u128::MAX`, I don't believe, but I wrote a test that does.

> Is there a way this match could have a catch-all arm that just does the "spit back what the user wrote", so that additions to the AST like this would do the right thing without needing a code change? 

Potentially. IIRC many of the big match expressions used to have a catch-all pattern arm in the earlier days when rustfmt used the AP crates, but that was intentionally changed some time back as part of an effort to ensure the rustfmt team would become aware of new syntax associated with new variants.

If/when we can implement some of the process changes previously discussed elsewhere then I'd certainly be open to adding such arms back. In the interim, for future reference, the behavior can be triggered with `None` or something like `Some(context.snippet(<<span>>).to_owned())`

author: oli-obk

body:
Crater says we're clean, but I think the other thing that helps is the direction in which we're using it -- `AsRef<T>` has this problem all the time when used as a method, because with `self` known then if there's only one impl it's easy to know the `T`.  But here we're going the other direction, and I know know that the inference engine allows taking advantage of "`FromResidual<Concrete>` is only implemented for one type", since that's fundamentally less stable, as anyone who wants *could* implement that themselves later if they want.

(And if there is a way that adding another impl here can be a breaking change, all the better to get a second one in early to keep anyone from finding a way to write stable code that depends on it 🙃)

This means that changing the system time can result in longer or shorter timeouts. I am currently looking through the Mach API to see whether there is a better ways to block, but the methods I could fine are all prone to racing between setting the park state and blocking.
author: joboet

I imagine most of the other ways that darwin offers (mach/libdispatch semaphores, for example) will be slower, because they're fair.
author: thomcc

body: The Mach semaphore supports setting a wakeup policy, so we can set that to FIFO. It also has `timedwait` with a relative timeout. I implemented a simple POC [here](https://gist.github.com/joboet/3758f6653f2dd28185e5af9b40f4d9d7).
author: joboet

body:
I don't know about that implementation. The FIFO policy is fair, I don't know that it makes sense to use fairness for the thread parker, and I suspect it is going to be slower than the current version (it's generally going to be the case that waking up a specific thread will be slower than waking up an arbitrary thread).

I also don't know that this actually is using a monotonic clock either. Is the clock used by the timedwait documented anywhere?
author: thomcc

body:
The `DispatchSemaphore` uses the Mach semaphore [internally](https://github.com/apple-oss-distributions/libdispatch/blob/55c3a68e9ec47f1c1d5bb9909404ce5f0351edef/src/shims/lock.c#L144-L162), and its timeout is relative to `mach_absolute_time` which we already use in `Instant` (see [`DispatchTime`](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/dispatch/dispatchtime)).

As to fairness and speed, I can't find any documentation suggesting the semaphore was fair, but the [Mach documentation](https://web.mit.edu/darwin/src/modules/xnu/osfmk/man/semaphore_create.html) suggests it does not consider priorities. In the end, the [semaphore messages](https://github.com/apple/darwin-xnu/blob/a1babec6b135d1f35b2590a1990af3c5c5393479/osfmk/kern/sync_sema.c#L704-L710) bottom out in the same wait-queue implementation [`ulock`](https://github.com/apple/darwin-xnu/blob/2ff845c2e033bd0ff64b5b6aa6063a1f8f65aa32/bsd/kern/sys_ulock.c#L709-L710) and thus the current `pthread` API uses, so the speed should be equivalent or faster, since we only use one synchronization primitive instead of two, assuming the message passing infrastructure is fast enough.
author: joboet

body:
Dispatch semaphore is also fair, and tends to be somewhat slow as a result (it's a tradeoff though).

EDIT: Note that fairness is not about priorities, it's about the order threads are woken in. That is, FIFO means fair, here.

`Eq`/`Hash` seem fine to add, though.

body:
It doesn't actually matter to me either way.

It shouldn't ever match anything, because we can work it out for the four possible ways you can get paths that look like that, and they're all impossible:

* `never::macro`: macros can currently only appear in modules, not primitives
* `never::never`: the Never primitive has no parent item
* `macro::macro`: macros have no child items
* `macro::never`: macros have no child items

However, I'm not sure we need to reject it in the parser. All that really matters to me is that the parser reject things that look like `!foo`, for future-proofing purposes. We might want to use that for exclusion syntax later (a NOT operator, basically).

body:
Shouldn't this visit the results in other direction?

The results are pre-calculated for each block entry point (start of a block for forward analysis, and end of a block for backward analysis). In addition to that the cursor stores the results for the current position.  Results for the current cursor position can be updated incrementally, but only when moving in the same direction as the analysis. Otherwise computation has to be restarted from the entry to the block.

<details><summary>An example with a large compile time regression</summary>

```console
$ cat a.py
#!/usr/bin/env python
import sys
n = int(sys.argv[1])
print('pub fn f<T>(a0: T) -> T {')
for i in range(n):
    print('  let a{}: T = a{};'.format(i + 1, i))
print('  a{}'.format(n))
print('}')
$ ./a.py 50000 > a.rs
$ time rustc --crate-type=lib a.rs -O
...
```
</details>

author: JakobDegen

body:
To avoid regressions in used stack space, I think we should merge storage ranges or deduce them for merged locals, before we can enable propagation by default.

Consider for example a match with a large number of arms. With storage markers the stack space would usually be a maximum over space required by each of the arms. With markers removed, even if the stack space required by each of the arms is now smaller, the total stack space required becomes additive over all arms.
author: tmiasko

body: I'm not so sure this is actually that likely, because we restrict to locals which never have a reference taken. These locals are overwhelmingly going to be mem2reg'd anyway, and LLVM *should* be able to recognize that the live ranges for these locals are unobservable anyway. As a matter of fact, if we had better benchmarks I'd want to test out just always removing storage statements for such locals
author: JakobDegen

body:
Even if a local never has its address taken at MIR level, this hardly reflects on what happens at LLVM IR level. If an alloca has no storage markers then as far as stack coloring is concerned it is always live.

The issue I mentioned earlier was originally reported for opt-level=0 builds. Using artificial reconstruction, I can now easily reproduce the issue in optimized builds with propagation enabled. Looking a few test cases from bug reports, I also see increased stack usage in some rustc parser code paths.

That is only anecdotal evidence, so I am ready to be convinced otherwise with more systematic measurements.
author: tmiasko

body:
Do you have an issue number?

For locals for which addresses are never observed in any meaningful way, LLVM opt should be able to insert liveness markers wherever appropriate. If it does not, then this is a pass that we should be adding to the MIR pipeline anyway, and is not specific to dest prop.
author: JakobDegen
tmiasko

body:
So the suggestion I mentioned above will still work as long as we have only locals that are never borrowed being considered in this optimization. But if/when that changes, this gets a lot more complicated. I think I know how to do it though, and am going to leave this comment here as documentation for that. Say we are attempting to merge `P` into `Q` (both locals). We merge storage live ranges via a two step algorithm:

 1. For each `X` among `P, Q` that is a never borrowed local: The storage liveness range of `X` can soundly be shrunk to its regular liveness range. In order for the next step of the algorithm to be effective, we insert storage statements to match this liveness range into the MIR explicitly.
 2. Now we do the following: We run `MaybeStorageLiveLocals`. For storage statement to `P`, we delete that statement if `Q` is maybe storage live, and the same thing the other way around. This is in essence the exact same algorithm we are already running for regular liveness - storage statements are a sort of "write" to the "liveness" of the local. The only thing that has changed is that in the case of the regular liveness, we do not perform the optimization if a write to one side conflicts with liveness on the other. In the case of storage liveness, we can instead just delete the write (by removing the storage statement).

The only problem with this approach is that it seems potentially quite slow. That'll require some engineering work ig
author: JakobDegen

This is a bad practice, `#![feature]` attributes are supposed to never or almost never change behavior, only to report or not report stability errors.

During stabilization the behavior will switch anyway, and not switching this earlier will only result in the feature having not enough coverage.

It should only be gated like this if it's not fully implemented and has known issues, but then it should have a FIXME explaining it.

body:
How deep do you plan to detect paths in the condition expression?
Will paths will be replaced with `{:?}`s even in items inside blocks with this approach?

If yes, then it's incorrect.
If no, then we are pulling assert-specific logic to the pretty-printer, which is also bad.

One alternative is to take a mutable AST visitor (`MutVisitor`) and replace all paths in `cond_expr` with some unique placeholder identifier up to the necessary depth (the identifier must be illegal in Rust lexical grammar though, otherwise it could occur in `cond_expr` naturally).
Then placeholders can be replaced with `{:?}`s by string replacement in the result of `pprust::expr_to_string`.

Also, what if `{:?}` occurs naturally in `cond_expr`? Then it should be escaped somehow.
author: petrochenkov

There are three things that still need to be implemented:

1. Constant environments
2. Avoid copying structures when methods don't take ownership. For example, `PartialEq`.
3. Prevent large copies when a method takes ownership. For example, `[u128; 4096]` -> https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=nightly&mode=debug&edition=2021&gist=85575be37ce9ebaa2286ba1a7ed4930a

Added all of them in a newly created FIXME
author: c410-f3r

body:
It may be possible to reliably detect `matches!`, it's just non-trivial.
- First we need to mark `macro_rules! matches` in the standard library as a lang item, diagnostic item, or whatever, so it's recognizable by the compiler.
- Then we need to support `ExpandResult::Retry` for fn-like built-in macros.
- Then we need to try resolving all macro paths inside `assert!`
    - If the resolution is indeterminate, abandon the whole `assert!` and return `ExpandResult::Retry` for it to be re-scheduled later, this may happen often because all of this happens during macro expansion.
    - If the resolution is determinate and matches the standard `matches`, then it's a success and we can parse the first argument as an expression.

So the main conclusion is, I guess, "not in this PR".

body:
This seems a little iffy to me -- I would have expected a single path in the output for a set on input. IOW, if we have e.g. set=[library/std, library/core, ...] then on output it probably makes sense to either keep just the first entry or have the vec (in the return type) here be a list of PathSets, not PathBufs.

In general the whole point of the PathSet abstraction was to let us have aliases without running multiple times. I suspect that krate -> all_krates is potentially the right move here, which puts all the dependencies into separate PathSets IIRC.
author: Mark-Simulacrum
jyn514

body:
> I suspect that krate -> all_krates is potentially the right move here, which puts all the dependencies into separate PathSets IIRC.

I actually want to get rid of `all_krates` completely, eventually, because it makes it impossible to do steps for a single crate, you *have* to do the whole step at once because you can't distinguish which one should be run.
author: jyn514

body:
> I actually want to get rid of all_krates completely, eventually, because it makes it impossible to do steps for a single crate, you have to do the whole step at once because you can't distinguish which one should be run.

I don't understand this -- maybe all_krates is confusing here.

To take a concrete step, let's call CrateLibrary the step for testing `library/{std,alloc,core}` (others too but for simplicity let's assume just these). I would expect that to configure (whether through all_krates or through some other mechanism) the  set of paths to have 3 separate pathsets (inventing aliases here, we may not actually want these particular ones): `{std, library/std}, {alloc, library/alloc}, {core, library/core}`.

If running by default (x.py test, no args), we'd want to invoke make_run with a vec of all three pathsets (since we have nothing more specific) (`vec![{std, library/std}, {alloc, library/alloc}, {core, library/core}]`), this should configure crates to be `[core, std, alloc]`.
For x.py test core library/std, we'd pass `vec![{std, library/std}, {core, library/core}]`.
For `x.py test --exclude core`, we'd pass `vec![{std, library/std}, {alloc, library/alloc}]`.

Does that make sense? Am I missing something?

It may be that passing the PathSet into run_make isn't *really* any better than just passing one of the elements, but in practice I think it's the right thing to do. In the future we might want PathSet to really represent something with an ID or whatever to make it easier to have the steps take Cargo-level metadata (crate IDs) -> human paths (for CLI invocation) -> Cargo-level metadata (crate IDs) in a way that round trips reliably and doesn't require as much guessing when looking things up in hashmaps.

But that can come later.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
> If running by default (x.py test, no args), we'd want to invoke make_run with a vec of all three pathsets (since we have nothing more specific) (vec![{std, library/std}, {alloc, library/alloc}, {core, library/core}]), this should configure crates to be [core, std, alloc].

ahh, I see - the "set" here is all possible aliases for the same crate (not the full Step - `all_krates` registers all aliases, but only with `Step` granularity).

I guess that doesn't hurt, but I'm not sure why it would be related to this PR? If you look at the `path` function I deleted, it also uses PathBuf and not PathSet, because nothing needs to know all possible aliases currently. I am kind of worried this overcomplicates the API - I would rather not build PathSet into this until and unless we end up using your ID scheme you mentioned in the last paragraph.

That said, it wouldn't be *that* much more complicated, so I'm ok with using PathSet instead if you feel strongly. But it would introduce additional work into each step having to go from the set to the path; and in most cases I think it will end up with `set.first().unwrap()` or similar which makes me a little nervous.
author: jyn514

body: I think the line I am commenting on previously retrieved the first element of the pathset, but it looks like it now takes all of the elements and pulls them in. That seems wrong to me; we should never flatten a PathSet (e.g., [{core, library/core}, {std, library/std}] -> [core, library/core, std, library/std]). Maybe I'm misreading the code though?
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
ah! ok, I see what's going on - for `PathSet::one`, like `krate` uses, this will be correct, but for `all_krates` the behavior is wrong, because it flattens aliases (and same for `ShouldRun::paths`).

I don't think any step that currently uses `all_crates` or `paths` actually uses `run.path` and I worry a little about changing the API too much to account for them. But I agree flattening is wrong - I will change the steps that actually use `run.path` to unwrap the path out of the pathset.
author: jyn514

body: Sorry this is so long and complicated. The tl;dr is no flattening is happening, but I happened to introduce a change in behavior when there are multiple pathsets matched for the same step.
author: jyn514

body:
Hm. So I think the rationale I'd expect is that we'd invoke a given Step once per PathSet, which I think corresponds to the new behavior (i.e., with run.paths having a full list). We'd still only actually run it once for most steps today since we cache steps (i.e., we invoke make_run multiple times but the step body is only run once).

I would expect it to be a hard requirement that we can work with multiple invocations of the same step across pathsets for things like x.py test library/std library/test to work, so that does seem like a blocker if we can't support that with this PR. If it's just the not-yet-"good" mode steps that are in all-crates-aliased-together mode that don't support it though that seems OK, we can largely ignore the issue as it's not really expected to come up in practice. I think adding just another line or something saying "or a previous rule already matched that step" would be fine there.

body: Wouldn't it make more sense to saturate with 0 for an infinite timeout?
author: Amanieu

body: I don't think it matters much, but I suppose technically sleeping shorter than requested is correct behaviour, while sleeping longer is not, so I 'round down' / saturate to i32::MAX. (We do have a place or two in `std` where we saturate a `timespec` to an infinite timeout, but reaching i32::MAX milliseconds (≈25 days) is still somewhat realistic, while reaching i32::MAX (≈70 years) or i64::MAX seconds (≈∞ years) is not.)
author: m-ou-se

body:
Now that I think about it, saturating to i32::MAX isn't really correct either, since in that case we shouldn't return `false` (`ETIMEDOUT`) when the timeout happens.

So we should either modify the return condition to include this situation as a spurious wakeup rather than a timeout, or just saturate to infinite for huge timeouts. What do you think?
author: m-ou-se

body: Just saturate to infinity: for all sleep operations the general rule is that we sleep *at least* as long as requested, but can sometimes sleep more due to system timer resolution limitations.

We don't include version numbers in the docs for every difference -- we host versioned copies of the docs (like <https://doc.rust-lang.org/1.57.0/std/macro.format.html>) so people using a particular version can see what that version supports.  And we don't need to document `panic!`'s edition behaviour here -- that's fine just in its own documentation.

# Editions

## 2021 and later

body:
If it affects MSVC, then I guess this is not only about DWARF? Even though LLVM basically uses DWARF in IR, it will translate that as needed -- looks like CodeView gets a `char32_t` encoding [here](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/9c8a88382d86c731db3c5c92d8ecd6ef296329ab/llvm/lib/CodeGen/AsmPrinter/CodeViewDebug.cpp#L1819-L1825).

Even for DWARF targets, `char` is not UTF-8, but UTF and a byte size attribute of 4 to represent UTF-32.
author: cuviper

body:
Yeah, this is a bit confusing. LLVM's debuginfo annotations are basically just DWARF and map very closely to it. For Windows, which doesn't use DWARF at all and instead uses CodeView records embedded in PDBs, LLVM walks the debug info it has at the end of the compilation session and attempts to generate similar CV records from it. In many cases, there isn't a good way to map the DWARF style debuginfo into CV records and LLVM will silently discard the debuginfo in these cases.

Prior to this change, there was no mapping into a primitive CV type for `DW_ATE_unsigned_char` and so the debuginfo related to `char` variables was silently discarded resulting in them being completely unknown to the debugger. LLDB has a similar problem where it didn't know how to display `DW_ATE_unsigned_char` and would report an error to the user instead of showing the value.

There is however a mapping for `DW_ATE_UTF` to primitive CV types so this change now causes LLVM to be able to generate the appropriate debuginfo for `char`s and LLDB also knows to display the value.

body:
This also needs a compatibility note.

Previously native static libraries were linked as whole-archive in some cases, now rustc tries to not use whole-archive unless explicitly requested.
This change may result in linking errors in some cases.
To fix such errors native libraries linked from build scripts or `#[link]` attributes need to
- (more common) either be reordered to respect dependencies between them (if `a` depends on `b` then `a` should go first and `b` second)
- (less common) or be updated to use the `+whole-archive` modifier.
author: petrochenkov

- https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/reference/items/external-blocks.html#the-link-attribute

body:
`proc_macro`-style tokens contain "sentences" (delimited groups) in them all the time, but for flattened `rustc_parse`-style tokens it's unusual, I guess.
When parser librarification was still in plans, one of the alternatives was to switch `rustc_parse` to the `proc_macro`-style token model.

The main reason to get rid of `Interpolated` tokens from my point of view is that we want to define everything happening during macro expansion in terms of token streams, so we have to treat the AST pieces as token streams at least in *some* cases, moreover the token stream representation is the source of truth for them.
So these AST pieces are supposed to be a parser caching mechanism at most, which is highly non-obvious when they are represented like this.

body:
#This also defeats the OOM protection, if we have for some reason rustc being over eager with *just* outputting paths. (Or repeating a path). It also won't avoid non-determinism in terms of failures here, at least not those caused by other differences in behavior (e.g., cfg(unix) vs not differences if testing on Windows).

Right now we abbreviate output after 416 KB of output from a single test. It seems like OOM-wise we should be comfortable bumping to, say, 5 megabytes -- I would expect any "reasonable" output from rustc to fit into that for our tests. That should make path length differences less of an issue, IMO, and is a much simpler change and feels less scary.

If we want to keep an eye on the tests so we catch cases where we dump kilobytes into the stderr for no good reason, I think our best bet would be a system closer to perf.rust-lang.org or toolstate; I could pretty easily imagine us dumping each test's output size (and maybe runtime?) into a file and then having a simple comparison post-merge that we're not increasing more than 10% (or staying in some <512KB range or something). I'm not really convinced we actually need to do anything here though.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
This approach won't really work, I think, at least not in general. The data we receive here is an ~arbitrary slice of the total test output, which means that there's no guarantee that the pattern we're looking for isn't lying across a boundary (i.e., half the pattern in one "chunk" and half in the next).

I think the way to mitigate this would be to start matching here pattern_bytes-1 into the already pushed data, but I'm not entirely confident on the full correctness of that approach. (It may also be worth considering a RegexSet here, from a performance perspective, to do a single pass over the incoming bytes. But with our hard upper bound being pretty small it's probably not worth it).

It's hard for me to conceptualize what the effect of that will be in the long run.

Maybe a better/simpler approach here is to set an upper bound on the excluded bytes (say, 2 MB, or so) which seems like it would more clearly express that while we do want to avoid different failures across different systems, we probably also want to avoid enormous outputs from rustc. I think the effect of the placeholder length here is probably pretty similar regardless (right?), just less obviously correct.

body:
The effect of this is that all paths will be considered 32 bytes long, regardless of whether they're shorter or longer. This should remove all path size differences between different machines.

I chose 32 bytes because that's the highest power of two that passes all the tests. Specifically for `src/test/ui/numeric/numeric-cast.rs`, all path lengths greater than 53 cause that test to fail. If you want the behavior to be more consistent with today I guess we could go with 48?
author: emilyalbini

body: Yeah, let's do that in a separate PR though.
author: emilyalbini

body:
Why do we need to subtract pattern_bytes from EXCLUDED_PLACEHOLDER_LEN though? It looks like we have a total match count (`matches`) and if we want to ensure that no matter what the pattern is we'd count it as 32 bytes, we'd just multiply by 32 (`EXCLUDED_PLACEHOLDER_LEN`) here, right?

32 is fine with this rationale, but I do think we should put a comment on its definition saying something like "Number of bytes any excluded segment of the input will be treated as", with perhaps a short note for why (cross-platform consistency).
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
> Why do we need to subtract pattern_bytes from EXCLUDED_PLACEHOLDER_LEN though? It looks like we have a total match count (matches) and if we want to ensure that no matter what the pattern is we'd count it as 32 bytes, we'd just multiply by 32 (EXCLUDED_PLACEHOLDER_LEN) here, right?

No, that'd still cause nondeterminism (`excluded_len` is the number of bytes that should be ignored from the output size).

The `pattern` is the path on the local filesystem (such as `/home/ci/rust`), so if we want to make things deterministic we need to ignore the full length of the pattern when counting the string size (so `matches * pattern_bytes.len()`).

That works, but it would lead to OOM if the compiler emits only the patterns without any extra bytes, as the whole output size would be ignored. To avoid that the code subtracts 32 from the pattern length: when the length of the pattern is lower than 32 the output will be considered *longer* (to simulate a system with longer paths), while when the length of the pattern is greater than 32 bytes it will be considered *shorter* (removing the nondeterminism, while preventing OOMs due to the 32 bytes still being counted).

In practice that's `32 - pattern_bytes` rather than `pattern_bytes - 32` due to the code below doing `len + excluded_bytes` rather than `len - excluded_bytes`.

I can change the code to invert signs (making things more clear) and add another comment explaining this.
author: emilyalbini

body:
Ah, I think part of my confusion was that pattern is a local file system path (rather than something more abstract like a regex, e.g., "/[^"]+)

I think we should maybe consider renaming it to something like path_prefix since it isn't really a pattern - or, at least, doesn't match more than one specific str.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body: I think the best thing here is to subtract `matches * pattern_bytes.len()` and then add `matches * 32`, which should I think be equivalent and much more obvious (to me, anyway), in terms of effect.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body: Pushed a commit addressing this.
author: emilyalbini

body:
I think this is going to run into some problems if we have overlapping matches (e.g., one of the patterns is /home/mark and the other is /home/mark/rust, then we'll subtract out twice).

I think src_base and build_base won't do that in practice so we're OK for now, but ideally we'd either (a) assert that it doesn't happen or (b) somehow fix it. I'm not quite sure what the best approach to fixing it would be -- trying to detect this kind of overlap seems annoying.

body:
Thanks for tackling this! It looks like basically the right approach, I just have a few comments on the implementation.

I also noticed that the `should_run` step is never actually hit, because `setup` is still special-cased in `Flags::parse`. Is it possible to remove that special-casing? You should double check that `x setup` without arguments still works (you probably will need to add `DEFAULT = true` in the Step impl) and that `x setup invalid` properly goes through the "no paths matched" path instead of "called unwrap on None".

One super cool thing to do is to still keep the description of each path, like it has currently:
```
Arguments:
    This subcommand accepts a 'profile' to use for builds. For example:

        ./x.py setup library

    The profile is optional and you will be prompted interactively if it is not given.
    The following profiles are available:

        library: Contribute to the standard library
        compiler: Contribute to the compiler itself
        codegen: Contribute to the compiler, and also modify LLVM or codegen
        tools: Contribute to tools which depend on the compiler, but do not modify it directly (e.g. rustdoc, clippy, miri)
        user: Install Rust from source
```
That will probably require modifying `ShouldRun` to allow passing in a description for the alias, though - no need to tackle it just now if it's tricky.

r? @Mark-Simulacrum just so this has someone assigned, but happy to be in charge of the review.

Yes, I agree this is not ideal at all, but I found that if no path is provided when running setup command, i.e. `x setup`, `StepDescrption`'s `maybe_run` will be called 5 times for each of the default profile. 

It seems the working way for me to make it run exactly once, no more no less, is to set this special-case here, to call `maybe_run` once, and not do the `for ... in` loop, for `setup`.

I actually spent quite a while thinking about how to achieve above without special-casing, please let me know if there is an alternative without special-casing!

body:
I just realized why this works - when `StepDescription::maybe_run` gets a `PathSet::empty()`, it will call `path()` on it which returns `builder.config.src`, which fails to parse. Then we try and parse it here, which fails because it's not a known profile. That seems ... very unintuitive. We should either
a) make it more clear that this is a hack by checking for `builder.config.src` explicitly and adding a comment, or
b) wait for https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/96501 so RunConfig has a `Vec<PathBuf>` instead of a single path, and we can pass in 0 paths.

My preference is for b.

author: bentongxyz

body:
It looks like the documentation on Step::should_run and Step::make_run is outdated, so that's probably a good idea to update as well (could be in this PR or a separate one).

I think the intent behind calling maybe_run for each of the pathsets configured by a step in should_run is that (historically) test::Crate and similar steps had differing behavior depending on the pathset they were called with, and would run through all the crates one by one with this logic. If we had called with an empty pathset, then presumably that would break those Steps. I think e.g. https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/96501 might affect this though, I'll need to sit down for some time and think through the interactions here I suppose and read the code a little more.

> [...] seems to assume that steps shouldn't care which path they're called with; but I don't see why that should be true?

I am not sure why you think that commit assumes this - indeed, the current logic I think rather goes to show the *opposite* which is that we must call with every path if we're invoking by default, rather than assuming all the paths are equivalent. (Well, not *every* path. Every path *set*, so aliased paths are only invoked with once).

Huh, a bit surprised this was using preorder. I thought it was RPO since that's the correct def-before-use order (but I guess we have to check for dominance anyway so this can't go wrong just be unnecessarily conservative?).

**EDIT**: ah I see, it went through these steps:
* `visit_body` -> inlined loop over blocks: #85965
* loop over blocks -> `preorder`: #85741
* `preorder` -> `reverse_postorder` (this PR)

Would it make sense to force `visit_body` to use RPO? Or have two forms, `visit_body_unordered` and `visit_body_rpo`?
author: eddyb

body: Note that preorder and reverse postorder give identical end results, since either visits a definition before a use, when the definition dominates the use (and the order is irrelevant otherwise).
author: tmiasko

body:
*Note: you can mostly ignore the rant below, it's me reasoning about RPO to myself, really*
Hmm, I think it depends on what kind of "definition" we're talking about - I think I overapproximated what RPO *actually did*, and how it's stronger than just giving you "dominator before dominated". I guess that implies a fun iteration algorithm like this: ```rust fn visit_block(&mut self, bb: Block) { if self.visited[bb] { return; } if let Some(dom_bb) = self.doms[bb] { self.visit_block(dom_bb); } // ... guaranteed to get here only once and *after* all dominators ... } ```
More seriously though, I guess RPO is important in SSA for merges (let's ignore cycles for now): ``` S(tart) / \ a b \ / M(erge) ``` |||flatten|dedup
(keep first)|dedup
(keep last)| |-|-:|-|-|-| ***pre*order**|`S->{a->M, b->M}`|`SaMbM`|`SaMb`|`SabM` | |**reverse** (*per-column*)|`MbMaS`|`bMaS`|`MbaS` ***post*order**|`{M<-a, M<-b}<-S`|`MaMbS`|`MabS`|`aMbS` | |**reverse** (*per-column*)|`SbMaM`|`SbaM`|`SbMa` And RPO is *usually* the reversed "dedup (keep first)" ***post*order**, which ends up as `SbaM` (though siblings can be reversed in the initial postorder visit to get `SabM` if that's *aesthetically* preferred for e.g. an IR dump - we should do this for `--emit=mir` IMO). What we're looking for in this example is `S->{a,b}->M` which is a bit like structured control-flow, and for SSA in particular it allows seeing *all* the definitions of φ nodes (or "BB args" values etc.) before a merge (not sure if this logic works for backedges, but it partially might?). In MIR we don't have φ nodes *but* we still want to see all "sources" of a merge *before* the merge for dataflow algorithms, for pretty much the same reason SSA IRs use φ/BB args, the difference being that a fixpoint dataflow algorithm is *only slowed down* by the suboptimal order (whereas SSA IR passes may have bigger issues with lacking definitions used by φ nodes). Also, you may have noticed the above table that the ***pre*order** "dedup (keep last)" is `SabM` *without* any reversals (but "keep last" is more expensive computationally, since we tend not to have the "flatten" form at all but instead skip visiting eagerly, which *naturally* results in "keep first"). So *really* what "RPO" does is a more efficient way to get "preorder but keep only the last visit instead of the usual first" (isomorphic up to sibling order, but I think you can get them perfectly equal if you make the "aesthetic fix" to RPO).
author: eddyb

body:
TLDR: Good catch! I will remove the `RuntimeError`.

Long note:
In python3  the traceback is stored in the exception:
```
$cat test.py
def foo():
	raise RuntimeError("foo error")
try:
	foo()
except RuntimeError:
	raise RuntimeError
$python3 test.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 5, in <module>
    foo()
  File "test.py", line 2, in foo
    raise RuntimeError("foo error")
RuntimeError: foo error

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 7, in <module>
    raise RuntimeError
RuntimeError
```

But the traceback is ugly. With just `raise` everything is nice and tidy
 ```
$cat test.py
def foo():
	raise RuntimeError("foo error")
try:
	foo()
except RuntimeError:
	raise
$python3 test.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 5, in <module>
    foo()
  File "test.py", line 2, in foo
    raise RuntimeError("foo error")
RuntimeError: foo error
```

Thanks!
author: TApplencourt

body:
So, this is fine, but looks wrong.

That is, if we ever would read through `self.addr` to get the underlying mutex, then Relaxed would be insufficient for this, due to it not ensuring data-dependencies (such as the writes to the value behind the pointer) are correctly ordered with respect to the load/store of the pointer. E.g. you'd need acquire/release semantics (although in principal if we had consume, consume/release would be fine). 

This would only matter on something like the Alpha though, but for better or worse, that's one of the targets that I've heard people want to use with rustc-codegen-gcc eventually.

Anyway, we don't do that here, so Relaxed should be fine. But as mentioned, it looks wrong (Relaxed is *almost* always wrong with AtomicPtr), so I think it needs a comment.

Perhaps something along the lines of "Relaxed is okay here because we never read through `self.addr`, and only use it to compare addresses", but you can feel free to reword if you'd like
author: thomcc

body: I started with an empty function here, and noticed an issue where `my_func` and `foobar` had been merged together by `mergefunc` pass, invalidating the reference from global asm. Probably `mergefunc` shouldn't touch symbols from `llvm.compiler.used`?
author: tmiasko

body: No, mergefunc is perfectly valid here. In general Rust doesn't guarantee that two functions will have separate addresses.
author: Amanieu

body: Since `collect_const_value` isn't doing its job here, you can just remove it entirely.
author: Amanieu

body: I mean, one of the functions is removed completely, and reference from the global asm is left unresolved.
author: tmiasko

body: Oh right, that does seem like a bug. If something is in `llvm.compiler.used` then it's likely that it is being referenced using its symbol name, in which case mergefuncs shouldn't rename it. I *think* we can work around this by removing `unnamed_addr` from any symbols referenced by `llvm.compiler.used` in a pass after all values are generated.
author: Amanieu

body:
I think this is might be related to symbol export. 

Functions that are referenced from inline asm in text form should be listed in `exported_symbols`. We currently don't have any special treatment of asm_sym there.
author: nbdd0121

body:
Could you give an example of what you have in mind?

As far as I know, for an inline assembly we do consider this aspect. For example, if a  function with an inline assembly block might be code generated in a different crate (because it is marked inline or generic) and it references a non-generic function, the referenced function will be exported as necessary, etc. In what situation would it be necessary in the case of global asm?
author: tmiasko

body: asm_sym is injected into the assembly as text, so when doing LTO/ThinLTO, LLVM would consider the symbol to be unused. This is different from `llvm.compiler.used`, which only marks the function to be used, not the symbol.
author: nbdd0121

body:
> This is different from `llvm.compiler.used`, which only marks the function to be used, not the symbol.

I see, but I think this is incorrect. Both `llvm.used` and `llvm.compiler.used` are about symbols, not function per se. The LLVM reference seems quite clear about that. Those lists are also limited to named values for that reason.
author: tmiasko

body: I reread LLVM doc, you are correct about that. Some further digging shows that the issue is actually we generate an LLVM IR that marks the function as `internal`: https://godbolt.org/z/c4jPajT48 -- removing the internal linkage will make codegen correct. The symbol export actually doesn't affect the outcome directly -- but it is used indirectly from rustc_monomorphize to determine the linkage.
author: nbdd0121

The source_map should only be used as a last resort when building diagnostics.

Can't we just check for it?
author: cjgillot
cjgillot

body:
@cjgillot 
Thank you for your review.

I think the case that a mod does not have the `#[cfg(test)]` attribute should show both warning and help.

```rust
mod tests {
    use std::collections::HashSet;

    #[test]
    fn test_empty() {}
}

fn main() {}
```

:o:
```bash
$ rustc test.rs
warning: unused import: ...
...
help: consider adding a `#[cfg(test)]` ...
```

:o:
```bash
$ rustc --test test.rs
warning: unused import: ...
...
help: consider adding a `#[cfg(test)]` ...
```

However, when we have a mod that has the attribute, then we should avoid emitting the help even if we are in the `--cfg test` mode.

```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use std::collections::HashSet;

    #[test]
    fn test_empty() {}
}

fn main() {}
```

:o:
```bash
$ rustc test.rs
# nothing
```

:x:
```bash
$ rustc --test test.rs
warning: unused import: ...
...
# ↓ incorrect: we want to suppress this help.
help: consider adding a `#[cfg(test)]` ...
```

So, if my understanding is correct, we still need to check if a mod has the attribute regardless of the `--cfg test` mode.

Could you please tell me if there is a way to implement this without using `source_map`?
author: ken-matsui

body:
Can we get `rustc_ast::ast::Item` from `parent_module`?

If so, we may retrieve the `#[cfg(test)]` attribute from [`attrs`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/beta/nightly-rustc/rustc_ast/ast/struct.Item.html#structfield.attrs), though.

body: The patching is not necessary on non-Linux systems. Patchelf doesn't work with Mach-O binaries like on macOS. On other unix systems there is a global system libc available that is not managed by Nix as other unix systems don't have a stable system call interface and thus only work with the system libc. Only on Linux can Nix manage libc and thus may have no global libc available.

body:
`Delimiter::Invisible` needs a more elaborate flag than just `skip: bool`, something like
```rust
enum InvisibleSource {
    Expr,
    Ty,
    Pat,
    ...
    Other,
}
```

All the uses of `NtExpr` in look ahead functions like `can_begin_*` will then be replaced with `OpenDelim(Delimiter::Invisible(InvisibleSource::Expr))` for compatibility.
We may be able to removed all these source flags later when everything else is ready, but it will require a crater run.

Also, `skip` is `source == Other`.

In that case the disambiguation between `parse_unsuffixed_lit` and `parse_meta_item` needs to be done at `parse_meta_item_inner` level.

It can be done with look ahead 1, if it's a `Invisible(ExprMv)` then it's a literal, otherwise it's a meta-item.
The whole idea of using `err.cancel()` for syntax disambiguation is a bad practice.

Looks like this expression gets its span from the "inner" expression, not from invisible parentheses.
It means that if its span is ever concatenated with other neighboring spans we'll get span regressions like those that are seen in tests.
Apparently the `lo` span used for concatenation was set to the metavar/invisible-parens span previously when `maybe_whole_expr` was used.

body:
That option attempts to prevent Windows getting clever with auto elevation. The default option for `MANIFESTUAC` is `asInvoker` which [means](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/build/reference/manifestuac-embeds-uac-information-in-manifest?view=msvc-170):

> The application runs at the same permission level as the process that started it. You can elevate the application to a higher permission level by selecting Run as Administrator.

I'm uncertain how necessary it is in the case of rustc but I generally use it as it leads to more predictable behaviour.
author: ChrisDenton

body: I've now made this more explicit and linked to the MSDN docs.
author: ChrisDenton

body:
> That option attempts to prevent Windows getting clever with auto elevation.

In what ways exactly? Auto elevation only happens for executables signed by microsoft, right?
author: bjorn3

body:
If the UAC option isn't declared then Windows uses certain heuristics to decide if a binary should be elevated. This will still prompt the user with the UAC dialogue so it's not quite the same as the elevation that happens with some Microsoft signed executables.

But in any case, it's unwanted so we may as well make that explicit, no?
author: ChrisDenton

body: According to https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/identity-protection/user-account-control/how-user-account-control-works the heuristics seem to be just for 32bit installer and uninstaller programs. I think all of the heuristics are deterministic in the sense that any given executable either triggers them or doesn't independent of the environment. Given that we haven't seen any issues about it being triggered I am pretty sure it will never trigger on rustc.exe.

body:
Didn't know the function existed, thanks! Feel free to file a PR.

Ideally, one would make an optimized implementation [like there is for str slices](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/f565016eddc3cb812e647d54b06cfe74bdee2900/library/alloc/src/str.rs#L87-L203), but `OsStr` is platform dependent in its representation as well as the invariants, so it's not as easy as just exporting that function from alloc, probably as perma unstable and `#[doc(hidden)]` and then using it, at least not on unixes. It's a larger project and I wasn't sure if the PR was going to accepted at all, so I went with the simplest implementation I could think of, establishing a baseline that can later be improved. *Maybe* a good next step would be to use the `join_generic_copy` function on Unix and fall back on the current implementation on other operating systems, but without benchmarking I don't know how much that is an improvement.

body:
I think this is not quite right -- Rust's standard licensing requires that contributions are licensed under *both* Apache and MIT, not one of them at the contributor's option, right? Otherwise *users* cannot choose one or the other, if there's code under just one of them, presumably?

(But maybe I have misunderstood).
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
So, I copied the wording from the top of this file: 

https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/c0672870491e84362f76ddecd50fa229f9b06dff/COPYRIGHT#L16-L19

I believe the wording here is correct as we want contributions to rustc_apfloat to be contributed under all of Apache, MIT, UIUC and to then allow the user to decide what license they will comply with.
author: wesleywiser

body: I think the problem is the "Contributions to the" prefix -- that is, the code is licensed under (Apache 2.0 or MIT or UIUC), that's correct, but *contributions* must be licensed under that full set of licenses -- contributors can't choose to only license their changes under MIT, for example.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
Agreed! I'm just struggling to see what other word would be appropriate here. The issue is that I'm specifically trying to make a distinction between the initial port and subsequent changes to that code. I can say "Subsequent changes to the library ..." but to my mind that means the same thing so it doesn't solve the issue. 

I'm interpreting "you" in this document to be a downstream user of Rust, not a contributor. As such, "at your option" is referring to options the user has not options a contributor has. Perhaps that's not the correct interpretation?
author: wesleywiser

body:
Yeah. I think it makes sense from you = user perspective, but then it's not entirely clear to me what users are expected to do with the 'based on' language. It also seems like we're needlessly(?) repeating ourselves -- MIT and Apache 2.0 are both listed twice and seemingly under the same "or" grouping from user's perspective, right?

Maybe we can change to something like:

> The rustc_apfloat crate, located in compiler/rustc_apfloat, is based on the APFloat library from a version of LLVM licensed under the UIUC license. Subsequent contributions to the rustc_apfloat crate are licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0, the MIT license, or the UIUC license, at your option.

I think the subsequent to me helps a little, as does the simplification away from having repeated licenses -- it makes the sentence structure simpler and overall gets the same message across, I believe.

I think the takeaway here is probably that it's probably worth doing some work on providing a "contributor copyright" file of some kind that basically requests licensing from contributors under some set of licenses. If possible, I think ideally we'd structure things so that we could omit UIUC from the list here, so that the story for contributors to current rust-lang/rust would be consistently "license all changes under both Apache 2.0 and MIT (each separately)", which seems better than having some folders also require other licenses to be 'granted'.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
> I think the subsequent to me helps a little, as does the simplification away from having repeated licenses -- it makes the sentence structure simpler and overall gets the same message across, I believe.

Agreed, it is much simpler! I believe the problem though is that until now, we've not asked that changes to this code be triple licensed and the advice Felix and I were given said this likely means those contributions were done under the normal Rust license (ie, only MIT/Apache2 only). Going forward, we're going to require contributors to `rustc_apfloat` to triple license under MIT/Apache2/UIUC but that still leaves the contributions which have already been made. As such, I don't think saying "Subsequent contributions to the `rustc_apfloat` crate are licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0, the MIT license, or the UIUC license, at your option." is entirely correct. 

We could certainly try to go back and get permission from contributors to license their code this way and that would simplify things here. I didn't want to block these improvements on that happening and it's possible we may not get every contributor to sign off on that. 

Per your other comment, I'm very much in agreement we should verify the specific changes with the Foundation but I'd like for us to decide what the changes should look prior to engaging with their lawyers again. 

Looking to the future, I see two potential paths to simplify our licensing here:

1. If we can secure relicensing consent from past contributors, we could simplify the licensing here to simply say "The `rustc_apfloat` crate is licensed under the UIUC license". That isn't ideal as it still deviates from our standard licensing but at least it is easy to explain and understand. 

2. We can perform a clean-room re-implementation of the `rustc_apfloat` crate based only on the type & function signatures of the current code. The code is quite self-contained and these should be quite feasible but Felix and I are not aware of anyone interested in taking this on. We'd also require some additional collaboration with the RF to make sure the re-implementation is done correctly and in a well documented way so there is no question as to the provenance of the code.
author: wesleywiser

body:
Yeah, seems like a complex situation. I agree on the right process here before re-engaging, let's decide on what would make us happy and work to get it approved.

I think what may be helpful - perhaps something worth asking - is the extent to which documenting history here is important. My relatively uninformed assumption is that ultimately consumers of the source code here probably can't do anything useful with even the simplified version I suggested - maybe the approach we should take is to have roughly these sentences:

* Based on UIUC code from LLVM.
* Initial changes made under Apache or MIT (as standard for other parts of the Rust distribution).
* Since YYYY-mm-dd, changes are trilicensed under Apache or MIT or UIUC.

But it's not entirely obvious to me that this is worthwhile - presumably to a consumer they'd only care about what they need to do when modifying/using/redistributing the code, so maybe we can try to approach things from that angle, though I don't really know what that would mean. If we can't get to a point where the code is available under just Apache 2.0 *or* MIT terms (and potentially other terms, but those are our standard), then it seems like we should probably make a best effort to get this language (perhaps in the terms I suggest, with explicit dates or commit SHAs) and then suggest reimplementing or so.

I don't think we need to worry about this. 
1. `validate_local` only checks the statement which an `local` first appears (and as an lvalue), so the `local` will not appear in the rvalue or the function parameters of this statement. 
2. During the validate_* process, `validate_local` is always executed when `local` is encountered, so their results are always the same.

body:
I think what we really need here is range attributes on parameters (which LLVM doesn't currently support).

Presumably, this PR generates IR like:
```ll
define @lookup_inc(..., i8 %f) {
  %0 = alloca i8
  store i8 %f, ptr %0
  %1 = load i8, ptr %0, !range !0
  ...
}

!0 = !{i8 0, i8 4}
```

But that will get optimized early on to something like:

```ll
define @lookup_inc(..., i8 %f) {
  %1 = %f
  ...
}
```
...dropping the range metadata on the floor.

One way to avoid this is to add assumes, like we did before. LLVM itself could also, in theory, add assumes during mem2reg to retain knowledge from allocas it drops.

But it seems like the real underlying problem is the lack of range attributes on parameters. If we could just generate IR like:

```ll
define @lookup_inc(..., i8 range(!0) %f) {
  ...
}

!0 = !{i8 0, i8 4}
```

...then we wouldn't need assumes or fragile `!range` metadata on alloca loads in the body of the function.

I will look into this, although I'm slightly worried that the reason `range` attributes aren't supported on parameters is because parameter attributes currently only accept 0 or 1 integer arguments, and it would take extensive refactoring to make them accept multiple arguments or reference a metadata node. Let's hope that's not the case.

body:
`fn create_object_file` is only used for creating object files that don't contain any actual code, only metadata or undefined symbol references.
I wonder which of these flags are really necessary.

It's clear that the ABI bits need to match between all the linked object files, otherwise you get that error above.
But what happens if you remove the PIC bits, for example? (Or arch, or nan, etc.)
author: petrochenkov

body:
I agree it shouldn't matter, but lld doesn't check if object files have no code before checking for mismatched flags (I assume for efficiency since you'd have to check every section of every object file).

Mismatched archs [fail here](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/main/lld/ELF/Arch/MipsArchTree.cpp#L277-L292), PIC [fails here](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/main/lld/ELF/Arch/MipsArchTree.cpp#L104-L114) and [NaN here](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/main/lld/ELF/Arch/MipsArchTree.cpp#L81-L84). FWIW the nan flag was [added after](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/commit/579e8bce7df948c786ab89f2a5d4b949cf44a44d) the initial PR for generating this object file, so I assume this has been an issue on targets other than the two I mentioned.

body:
This looks terrific to me. :) Thanks. I concur with all of the design decisions justified in https://github.com/rust-lang/libs-team/issues/60:

- the idea that these should exist at all, rather than doing CAS loops in the downstream code
- element-sized offsetting by itself is not sufficient
- byte-sized offsetting by itself would be annoying (but I'm sure we'll revisit this possibility when stabilizing)
- the specific naming scheme `fetch_ptr_add` and `fetch_byte_add`
- `atomptr.fetch_ptr_add(n, ord).add(n)` as the recommendation for non-wrapping semantics

The last would be good to document as an idiom in the part of the documentation that talks about `fetch_ptr_add`'s relationship with `wrapping_add`, or maybe in the example code. r=me if you get to it before bors does, otherwise separate PR or leave to the stabilization PR.

Can someone check that this is valid signature? [llvm docs](https://llvm.org/docs/LangRef.html#llvm-ptrmask-intrinsic) give the following signature:
```llvm
declare ptrty llvm.ptrmask(ptrty %ptr, intty %mask) readnone speculatable
```
And the rust intrinsic has the following signature:
```rust
fn ptr_mask<T>(ptr: *const T, mask: usize) -> *const T;
```

Are all of these compatible with each other?

body:
So the llvm-ir looks like this:
```llvm
; Function Attrs: nonlazybind uwtable
define i8* @mask_ptr(i8* %ptr, i64 %mask) unnamed_addr #0 {
start:
  %0 = alloca i8*, align 8
  %1 = bitcast i8* %ptr to void*
  %2 = call void* @llvm.ptrmask.p0isVoid.i64(void* %1, i64 %mask)
  %3 = bitcast i8** %0 to void**
  store void* %2, void** %3, align 8
  %4 = load i8*, i8** %0, align 8
  br label %bb1

bb1:                                              ; preds = %start
  ret i8* %4
}
```

Interestingly enough llvm seems to be casting the pointer to `void*` and only then using the intrinsic. Should I do the following?
```rust
// CHECK-LABEL: @mask_ptr
// CHECK-SAME: [[WORD:i[0-9]+]] %mask
#[no_mangle]
pub fn mask_ptr(ptr: *const u8, mask: usize) -> *const u8 {
    // CHECK: call
    // CHECK-SAME: @llvm.ptrmask.p0isVoid.[[WORD]](
    core::intrinsics::ptr_mask(ptr, mask)
}
```
author: WaffleLapkin

body: Ah, sure, if that's what the name is, that looks good.  Making it clear exactly what the real name that'll be used is -- especially when my understanding was wrong! -- is exactly what I'm hoping for with the test 🙂
author: scottmcm

body: cc @nikic, actually -- should it be `@llvm.ptrmask.{{ptr|p0isVoid}}.[[WORD]](` to make sure not to fail in the [opaque pointers](https://llvm.org/docs/OpaquePointers.html) future?
author: scottmcm

body:
I'm unsure: Should this maybe be something like
```suggestion
    // CHECK-SAME: @llvm.ptrmask.{{p0|p0i8}}.[[WORD]]({{ptr|i8\*}} %ptr, [[WORD]] %mask)
```
to make it not fail on older LLVM that's (presumably) not using opaque pointers?
author: scottmcm
